entries.name: AlignMode
entries.description: Text alignment.

constants.name: center
constants.description: Align text center.

constants.name: left
constants.description: Align text left.

constants.name: right
constants.description: Align text right.

constants.name: justify
constants.description: Align text both left and right.

entries.name: ArcType
entries.description: Different types of arcs that can be drawn.

constants.name: pie
constants.description: The arc is drawn like a slice of pie, with the arc circle connected to the center at its end-points.

constants.name: open
constants.description: The arc circle's two end-points are unconnected when the arc is drawn as a line. Behaves like the "closed" arc type when the arc is drawn in filled mode.

constants.name: closed
constants.description: The arc circle's two end-points are connected to each other.

entries.name: AreaSpreadDistribution
entries.description: Types of particle area spread distribution.

constants.name: uniform
constants.description: Uniform distribution.

constants.name: normal
constants.description: Normal (gaussian) distribution.

constants.name: ellipse
constants.description: Uniform distribution in an ellipse.

constants.name: borderellipse
constants.description: Distribution in an ellipse with particles spawning at the edges of the ellipse.

constants.name: borderrectangle
constants.description: Distribution in a rectangle with particles spawning at the edges of the rectangle.

constants.name: none
constants.description: No distribution - area spread is disabled.

entries.name: BezierCurve
entries.description:
A Bézier curve object that can evaluate and render Bézier curves of arbitrary degree.

For more information on Bézier curves check this great article on Wikipedia.

entries.name: evaluate
entries.description:
Evaluate Bézier curve at parameter t. The parameter must be between 0 and 1 (inclusive).

This function can be used to move objects along paths or tween parameters. However it should not be used to render the curve, see BezierCurve:render for that purpose.

arguments.name: t
arguments.description: Where to evaluate the curve.

returns.name: x
returns.description: x coordinate of the curve at parameter t.

returns.name: y
returns.description: y coordinate of the curve at parameter t.

entries.name: getControlPoint
entries.description: Get coordinates of the i-th control point. Indices start with 1.

arguments.name: i
arguments.description: Index of the control point.

returns.name: x
returns.description: Position of the control point along the x axis.

returns.name: y
returns.description: Position of the control point along the y axis.

entries.name: getControlPointCount
entries.description: Get the number of control points in the Bézier curve.

returns.name: count
returns.description: The number of control points.

entries.name: getDegree
entries.description: Get degree of the Bézier curve. The degree is equal to number-of-control-points - 1.

returns.name: degree
returns.description: Degree of the Bézier curve.

entries.name: getDerivative
entries.description:
Get the derivative of the Bézier curve.

This function can be used to rotate sprites moving along a curve in the direction of the movement and compute the direction perpendicular to the curve at some parameter t.

returns.name: derivative
returns.description: The derivative curve.

entries.name: getSegment
entries.description: Gets a BezierCurve that corresponds to the specified segment of this BezierCurve.

arguments.name: startpoint
arguments.description: The starting point along the curve. Must be between 0 and 1.

arguments.name: endpoint
arguments.description: The end of the segment. Must be between 0 and 1.

returns.name: curve
returns.description: A BezierCurve that corresponds to the specified segment.

entries.name: insertControlPoint
entries.description: Insert control point as the new i-th control point. Existing control points from i onwards are pushed back by 1. Indices start with 1. Negative indices wrap around: -1 is the last control point, -2 the one before the last, etc.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: Position of the control point along the x axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: Position of the control point along the y axis.

arguments.name: i
arguments.description: Index of the control point.

entries.name: removeControlPoint
entries.description: Removes the specified control point.

arguments.name: index
arguments.description: The index of the control point to remove.

entries.name: render
entries.description:
Get a list of coordinates to be used with love.graphics.line.

This function samples the Bézier curve using recursive subdivision. You can control the recursion depth using the depth parameter.

If you are just interested to know the position on the curve given a parameter, use BezierCurve:evaluate.

arguments.name: depth
arguments.description: Number of recursive subdivision steps.

returns.name: coordinates
returns.description: List of x,y-coordinate pairs of points on the curve.

entries.name: renderSegment
entries.description:
Get a list of coordinates on a specific part of the curve, to be used with love.graphics.line.

This function samples the Bézier curve using recursive subdivision. You can control the recursion depth using the depth parameter.

If you are just need to know the position on the curve given a parameter, use BezierCurve:evaluate.

arguments.name: startpoint
arguments.description: The starting point along the curve. Must be between 0 and 1.

arguments.name: endpoint
arguments.description: The end of the segment to render. Must be between 0 and 1.

arguments.name: depth
arguments.description: Number of recursive subdivision steps.

returns.name: coordinates
returns.description: List of x,y-coordinate pairs of points on the specified part of the curve.

entries.name: rotate
entries.description: Rotate the Bézier curve by an angle.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: Rotation angle in radians.

arguments.name: ox
arguments.description: X coordinate of the rotation center.

arguments.name: oy
arguments.description: Y coordinate of the rotation center.

entries.name: scale
entries.description: Scale the Bézier curve by a factor.

arguments.name: s
arguments.description: Scale factor.

arguments.name: ox
arguments.description: X coordinate of the scaling center.

arguments.name: oy
arguments.description: Y coordinate of the scaling center.

entries.name: setControlPoint
entries.description: Set coordinates of the i-th control point. Indices start with 1.

arguments.name: i
arguments.description: Index of the control point.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: Position of the control point along the x axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: Position of the control point along the y axis.

entries.name: translate
entries.description: Move the Bézier curve by an offset.

arguments.name: dx
arguments.description: Offset along the x axis.

arguments.name: dy
arguments.description: Offset along the y axis.

entries.name: BlendAlphaMode
entries.description: Different ways alpha affects color blending. See BlendMode and the BlendMode Formulas for additional notes.

constants.name: alphamultiply
constants.description: The RGB values of what's drawn are multiplied by the alpha values of those colors during blending. This is the default alpha mode.

constants.name: premultiplied
constants.description: The RGB values of what's drawn are '''not''' multiplied by the alpha values of those colors during blending. For most blend modes to work correctly with this alpha mode, the colors of a drawn object need to have had their RGB values multiplied by their alpha values at some point previously ("premultiplied alpha").

entries.name: BlendMode
entries.description: Different ways to do color blending. See BlendAlphaMode and the BlendMode Formulas for additional notes.

constants.name: alpha
constants.description: Alpha blending (normal). The alpha of what's drawn determines its opacity.

constants.name: replace
constants.description: The colors of what's drawn completely replace what was on the screen, with no additional blending. The BlendAlphaMode specified in love.graphics.setBlendMode still affects what happens.

constants.name: screen
constants.description: 'Screen' blending.

constants.name: add
constants.description: The pixel colors of what's drawn are added to the pixel colors already on the screen. The alpha of the screen is not modified.

constants.name: subtract
constants.description: The pixel colors of what's drawn are subtracted from the pixel colors already on the screen. The alpha of the screen is not modified.

constants.name: multiply
constants.description: The pixel colors of what's drawn are multiplied with the pixel colors already on the screen (darkening them). The alpha of drawn objects is multiplied with the alpha of the screen rather than determining how much the colors on the screen are affected, even when the "alphamultiply" BlendAlphaMode is used.

constants.name: lighten
constants.description: The pixel colors of what's drawn are compared to the existing pixel colors, and the larger of the two values for each color component is used. Only works when the "premultiplied" BlendAlphaMode is used in love.graphics.setBlendMode.

constants.name: darken
constants.description: The pixel colors of what's drawn are compared to the existing pixel colors, and the smaller of the two values for each color component is used. Only works when the "premultiplied" BlendAlphaMode is used in love.graphics.setBlendMode.

constants.name: additive
constants.description: Additive blend mode.

constants.name: subtractive
constants.description: Subtractive blend mode.

constants.name: multiplicative
constants.description: Multiply blend mode.

constants.name: premultiplied
constants.description: Premultiplied alpha blend mode.

entries.name: Body
entries.description: Bodies are objects with velocity and position.

entries.name: applyAngularImpulse
entries.description:
Applies an angular impulse to a body. This makes a single, instantaneous addition to the body momentum.

A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction does '''not''' depend on the timestep, and is equivalent to applying a force continuously for 1 second. Impulses are best used to give a single push to a body. For a continuous push to a body it is better to use Body:applyForce.

arguments.name: impulse
arguments.description: The impulse in kilogram-square meter per second.

entries.name: applyForce
entries.description:
Apply force to a Body.

A force pushes a body in a direction. A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction also depends on how long a force is applied: since the force acts continuously over the entire timestep, a short timestep will only push the body for a short time. Thus forces are best used for many timesteps to give a continuous push to a body (like gravity). For a single push that is independent of timestep, it is better to use Body:applyLinearImpulse.

If the position to apply the force is not given, it will act on the center of mass of the body. The part of the force not directed towards the center of mass will cause the body to spin (and depends on the rotational inertia).

Note that the force components and position must be given in world coordinates.

arguments.name: fx
arguments.description: The x component of force to apply to the center of mass.

arguments.name: fy
arguments.description: The y component of force to apply to the center of mass.

arguments.name: fx
arguments.description: The x component of force to apply.

arguments.name: fy
arguments.description: The y component of force to apply.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to apply the force.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to apply the force.

entries.name: applyLinearImpulse
entries.description:
Applies an impulse to a body.

This makes a single, instantaneous addition to the body momentum.

An impulse pushes a body in a direction. A body with with a larger mass will react less. The reaction does '''not''' depend on the timestep, and is equivalent to applying a force continuously for 1 second. Impulses are best used to give a single push to a body. For a continuous push to a body it is better to use Body:applyForce.

If the position to apply the impulse is not given, it will act on the center of mass of the body. The part of the impulse not directed towards the center of mass will cause the body to spin (and depends on the rotational inertia). 

Note that the impulse components and position must be given in world coordinates.

arguments.name: ix
arguments.description: The x component of the impulse applied to the center of mass.

arguments.name: iy
arguments.description: The y component of the impulse applied to the center of mass.

arguments.name: ix
arguments.description: The x component of the impulse.

arguments.name: iy
arguments.description: The y component of the impulse.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to apply the impulse.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to apply the impulse.

entries.name: applyTorque
entries.description:
Apply torque to a body.

Torque is like a force that will change the angular velocity (spin) of a body. The effect will depend on the rotational inertia a body has.

arguments.name: torque
arguments.description: The torque to apply.

entries.name: destroy
entries.description:
Explicitly destroys the Body and all fixtures and joints attached to it.

An error will occur if you attempt to use the object after calling this function. In 0.7.2, when you don't have time to wait for garbage collection, this function may be used to free the object immediately.

entries.name: getAngle
entries.description:
Get the angle of the body.

The angle is measured in radians. If you need to transform it to degrees, use math.deg.

A value of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes ''clockwise'' from our point of view.

returns.name: angle
returns.description: The angle in radians.

entries.name: getAngularDamping
entries.description:
Gets the Angular damping of the Body

The angular damping is the ''rate of decrease of the angular velocity over time'': A spinning body with no damping and no external forces will continue spinning indefinitely. A spinning body with damping will gradually stop spinning.

Damping is not the same as friction - they can be modelled together. However, only damping is provided by Box2D (and LOVE).

Damping parameters should be between 0 and infinity, with 0 meaning no damping, and infinity meaning full damping. Normally you will use a damping value between 0 and 0.1.

returns.name: damping
returns.description: The value of the angular damping.

entries.name: getAngularVelocity
entries.description:
Get the angular velocity of the Body.

The angular velocity is the ''rate of change of angle over time''.

It is changed in World:update by applying torques, off centre forces/impulses, and angular damping. It can be set directly with Body:setAngularVelocity.

If you need the ''rate of change of position over time'', use Body:getLinearVelocity.

returns.name: w
returns.description: The angular velocity in radians/second.

entries.name: getContacts
entries.description: Gets a list of all Contacts attached to the Body.

returns.name: contacts
returns.description: A list with all contacts associated with the Body.

entries.name: getFixtures
entries.description: Returns a table with all fixtures.

returns.name: fixtures
returns.description: A sequence with all fixtures.

entries.name: getGravityScale
entries.description: Returns the gravity scale factor.

returns.name: scale
returns.description: The gravity scale factor.

entries.name: getInertia
entries.description:
Gets the rotational inertia of the body.

The rotational inertia is how hard is it to make the body spin.

returns.name: inertia
returns.description: The rotational inertial of the body.

entries.name: getJoints
entries.description: Returns a table containing the Joints attached to this Body.

returns.name: joints
returns.description: A sequence with the Joints attached to the Body.

entries.name: getLinearDamping
entries.description:
Gets the linear damping of the Body.

The linear damping is the ''rate of decrease of the linear velocity over time''. A moving body with no damping and no external forces will continue moving indefinitely, as is the case in space. A moving body with damping will gradually stop moving.

Damping is not the same as friction - they can be modelled together.

returns.name: damping
returns.description: The value of the linear damping.

entries.name: getLinearVelocity
entries.description:
Gets the linear velocity of the Body from its center of mass.

The linear velocity is the ''rate of change of position over time''.

If you need the ''rate of change of angle over time'', use Body:getAngularVelocity.

If you need to get the linear velocity of a point different from the center of mass:

*  Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint allows you to specify the point in local coordinates.

*  Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint allows you to specify the point in world coordinates.

See page 136 of 'Essential Mathematics for Games and Interactive Applications' for definitions of local and world coordinates.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the velocity vector

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the velocity vector

entries.name: getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint
entries.description:
Get the linear velocity of a point on the body.

The linear velocity for a point on the body is the velocity of the body center of mass plus the velocity at that point from the body spinning.

The point on the body must given in local coordinates. Use Body:getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint to specify this with world coordinates.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to measure velocity.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to measure velocity.

returns.name: vx
returns.description: The x component of velocity at point (x,y).

returns.name: vy
returns.description: The y component of velocity at point (x,y).

entries.name: getLinearVelocityFromWorldPoint
entries.description:
Get the linear velocity of a point on the body.

The linear velocity for a point on the body is the velocity of the body center of mass plus the velocity at that point from the body spinning.

The point on the body must given in world coordinates. Use Body:getLinearVelocityFromLocalPoint to specify this with local coordinates.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position to measure velocity.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position to measure velocity.

returns.name: vx
returns.description: The x component of velocity at point (x,y).

returns.name: vy
returns.description: The y component of velocity at point (x,y).

entries.name: getLocalCenter
entries.description:
Get the center of mass position in local coordinates.

Use Body:getWorldCenter to get the center of mass in world coordinates.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x coordinate of the center of mass.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y coordinate of the center of mass.

entries.name: getLocalPoint
entries.description: Transform a point from world coordinates to local coordinates.

arguments.name: worldX
arguments.description: The x position in world coordinates.

arguments.name: worldY
arguments.description: The y position in world coordinates.

returns.name: localX
returns.description: The x position in local coordinates.

returns.name: localY
returns.description: The y position in local coordinates.

entries.name: getLocalPoints
entries.description: Transforms multiple points from world coordinates to local coordinates.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: (Argument) The x position of the first point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: (Argument) The y position of the first point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: (Argument) The x position of the second point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: (Argument) The y position of the second point.
(Argument) You can continue passing x and y position of the points.

returns.name: x1
returns.description: (Result) The transformed x position of the first point.

returns.name: y1
returns.description: (Result) The transformed y position of the first point.

returns.name: x2
returns.description: (Result) The transformed x position of the second point.

returns.name: y2
returns.description: (Result) The transformed y position of the second point.
(Result) Additional transformed x and y position of the points.

entries.name: getLocalVector
entries.description: Transform a vector from world coordinates to local coordinates.

arguments.name: worldX
arguments.description: The vector x component in world coordinates.

arguments.name: worldY
arguments.description: The vector y component in world coordinates.

returns.name: localX
returns.description: The vector x component in local coordinates.

returns.name: localY
returns.description: The vector y component in local coordinates.

entries.name: getMass
entries.description:
Get the mass of the body.

Static bodies always have a mass of 0.

returns.name: mass
returns.description: The mass of the body (in kilograms).

entries.name: getMassData
entries.description: Returns the mass, its center, and the rotational inertia.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x position of the center of mass.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y position of the center of mass.

returns.name: mass
returns.description: The mass of the body.

returns.name: inertia
returns.description: The rotational inertia.

entries.name: getPosition
entries.description:
Get the position of the body.

Note that this may not be the center of mass of the body.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x position.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y position.

entries.name: getTransform
entries.description:
Get the position and angle of the body.

Note that the position may not be the center of mass of the body. An angle of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes clockwise from our point of view.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x component of the position.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y component of the position.

returns.name: angle
returns.description: The angle in radians.

entries.name: getType
entries.description: Returns the type of the body.

returns.name: type
returns.description: The body type.

entries.name: getUserData
entries.description: Returns the Lua value associated with this Body.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The Lua value associated with the Body.

entries.name: getWorld
entries.description: Gets the World the body lives in.

returns.name: world
returns.description: The world the body lives in.

entries.name: getWorldCenter
entries.description:
Get the center of mass position in world coordinates.

Use Body:getLocalCenter to get the center of mass in local coordinates.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x coordinate of the center of mass.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y coordinate of the center of mass.

entries.name: getWorldPoint
entries.description: Transform a point from local coordinates to world coordinates.

arguments.name: localX
arguments.description: The x position in local coordinates.

arguments.name: localY
arguments.description: The y position in local coordinates.

returns.name: worldX
returns.description: The x position in world coordinates.

returns.name: worldY

entries.name: getWorldPoints

arguments.name: x1

arguments.name: y1

arguments.name: x2

returns.name: y1
returns.description: The transformed y position of the first point.

entries.name: getWorldVector
entries.description: Transform a vector from local coordinates to world coordinates.

arguments.name: localX
arguments.description: The vector x component in local coordinates.

arguments.name: localY
arguments.description: The vector y component in local coordinates.

returns.name: worldX
returns.description: The vector x component in world coordinates.

returns.name: worldY
returns.description: The vector y component in world coordinates.

entries.name: getX
entries.description: Get the x position of the body in world coordinates.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x position in world coordinates.

entries.name: getY
entries.description: Get the y position of the body in world coordinates.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y position in world coordinates.

entries.name: isActive
entries.description: Returns whether the body is actively used in the simulation.

returns.name: status
returns.description: True if the body is active or false if not.

entries.name: isAwake
entries.description: Returns the sleep status of the body.

returns.name: status
returns.description: True if the body is awake or false if not.

entries.name: isBullet
entries.description:
Get the bullet status of a body.

There are two methods to check for body collisions:

*  at their location when the world is updated (default)

*  using continuous collision detection (CCD)

The default method is efficient, but a body moving very quickly may sometimes jump over another body without producing a collision. A body that is set as a bullet will use CCD. This is less efficient, but is guaranteed not to jump when moving quickly.

Note that static bodies (with zero mass) always use CCD, so your walls will not let a fast moving body pass through even if it is not a bullet.

returns.name: status
returns.description: The bullet status of the body.

entries.name: isDestroyed
entries.description: Gets whether the Body is destroyed. Destroyed bodies cannot be used.

returns.name: destroyed
returns.description: Whether the Body is destroyed.

entries.name: isFixedRotation
entries.description: Returns whether the body rotation is locked.

returns.name: fixed
returns.description: True if the body's rotation is locked or false if not.

entries.name: isSleepingAllowed
entries.description: Returns the sleeping behaviour of the body.

returns.name: allowed
returns.description: True if the body is allowed to sleep or false if not.

entries.name: isTouching
entries.description: Gets whether the Body is touching the given other Body.

arguments.name: otherbody
arguments.description: The other body to check.

returns.name: touching
returns.description: True if this body is touching the other body, false otherwise.

entries.name: resetMassData
entries.description: Resets the mass of the body by recalculating it from the mass properties of the fixtures.

entries.name: setActive
entries.description:
Sets whether the body is active in the world.

An inactive body does not take part in the simulation. It will not move or cause any collisions.

arguments.name: active
arguments.description: If the body is active or not.

entries.name: setAngle
entries.description:
Set the angle of the body.

The angle is measured in radians. If you need to transform it from degrees, use math.rad.

A value of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes ''clockwise'' from our point of view.

It is possible to cause a collision with another body by changing its angle.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: The angle in radians.

entries.name: setAngularDamping
entries.description:
Sets the angular damping of a Body

See Body:getAngularDamping for a definition of angular damping.

Angular damping can take any value from 0 to infinity. It is recommended to stay between 0 and 0.1, though. Other values will look unrealistic.

arguments.name: damping
arguments.description: The new angular damping.

entries.name: setAngularVelocity
entries.description:
Sets the angular velocity of a Body.

The angular velocity is the ''rate of change of angle over time''.

This function will not accumulate anything; any impulses previously applied since the last call to World:update will be lost.

arguments.name: w
arguments.description: The new angular velocity, in radians per second

entries.name: setAwake
entries.description: Wakes the body up or puts it to sleep.

arguments.name: awake
arguments.description: The body sleep status.

entries.name: setBullet
entries.description:
Set the bullet status of a body.

There are two methods to check for body collisions:

*  at their location when the world is updated (default)

*  using continuous collision detection (CCD)

The default method is efficient, but a body moving very quickly may sometimes jump over another body without producing a collision. A body that is set as a bullet will use CCD. This is less efficient, but is guaranteed not to jump when moving quickly.

Note that static bodies (with zero mass) always use CCD, so your walls will not let a fast moving body pass through even if it is not a bullet.

arguments.name: status
arguments.description: The bullet status of the body.

entries.name: setFixedRotation
entries.description:
Set whether a body has fixed rotation.

Bodies with fixed rotation don't vary the speed at which they rotate. Calling this function causes the mass to be reset.

arguments.name: isFixed
arguments.description: Whether the body should have fixed rotation.

entries.name: setGravityScale
entries.description: Sets a new gravity scale factor for the body.

arguments.name: scale
arguments.description: The new gravity scale factor.

entries.name: setInertia
entries.description: Set the inertia of a body.

arguments.name: inertia
arguments.description: The new moment of inertia, in kilograms * pixel squared.

entries.name: setLinearDamping
entries.description:
Sets the linear damping of a Body

See Body:getLinearDamping for a definition of linear damping.

Linear damping can take any value from 0 to infinity. It is recommended to stay between 0 and 0.1, though. Other values will make the objects look 'floaty'(if gravity is enabled).

arguments.name: ld
arguments.description: The new linear damping

entries.name: setLinearVelocity
entries.description:
Sets a new linear velocity for the Body.

This function will not accumulate anything; any impulses previously applied since the last call to World:update will be lost.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-component of the velocity vector.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-component of the velocity vector.

entries.name: setMass
entries.description: Sets a new body mass.

arguments.name: mass
arguments.description: The mass, in kilograms.

entries.name: setMassData
entries.description: Overrides the calculated mass data.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the center of mass.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the center of mass.

arguments.name: mass
arguments.description: The mass of the body.

arguments.name: inertia
arguments.description: The rotational inertia.

entries.name: setPosition
entries.description:
Set the position of the body.

Note that this may not be the center of mass of the body.

This function cannot wake up the body.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position.

entries.name: setSleepingAllowed
entries.description: Sets the sleeping behaviour of the body. Should sleeping be allowed, a body at rest will automatically sleep. A sleeping body is not simulated unless it collided with an awake body. Be wary that one can end up with a situation like a floating sleeping body if the floor was removed.

arguments.name: allowed
arguments.description: True if the body is allowed to sleep or false if not.

entries.name: setTransform
entries.description:
Set the position and angle of the body.

Note that the position may not be the center of mass of the body. An angle of 0 radians will mean 'looking to the right'. Although radians increase counter-clockwise, the y axis points down so it becomes clockwise from our point of view.

This function cannot wake up the body.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x component of the position.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y component of the position.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: The angle in radians.

entries.name: setType
entries.description: Sets a new body type.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: The new type.

entries.name: setUserData
entries.description:
Associates a Lua value with the Body.

To delete the reference, explicitly pass nil.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The Lua value to associate with the Body.

entries.name: setX
entries.description:
Set the x position of the body.

This function cannot wake up the body.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position.

entries.name: setY
entries.description:
Set the y position of the body.

This function cannot wake up the body.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position.

entries.name: BodyType
entries.description: The types of a Body.

constants.name: static
constants.description: Static bodies do not move.

constants.name: dynamic
constants.description: Dynamic bodies collide with all bodies.

constants.name: kinematic
constants.description: Kinematic bodies only collide with dynamic bodies.

entries.name: BufferMode
entries.description: Buffer modes for File objects.

constants.name: none
constants.description: No buffering. The result of write and append operations appears immediately.

constants.name: line
constants.description: Line buffering. Write and append operations are buffered until a newline is output or the buffer size limit is reached.

constants.name: full
constants.description: Full buffering. Write and append operations are always buffered until the buffer size limit is reached.

entries.name: ByteData
entries.description:
Data object containing arbitrary bytes in an contiguous memory.

There are currently no LÖVE functions provided for manipulating the contents of a ByteData, but Data:getPointer can be used with LuaJIT's FFI to access and write to the contents directly.

entries.name: Canvas
entries.description:
A Canvas is used for off-screen rendering. Think of it as an invisible screen that you can draw to, but that will not be visible until you draw it to the actual visible screen. It is also known as "render to texture".

By drawing things that do not change position often (such as background items) to the Canvas, and then drawing the entire Canvas instead of each item,  you can reduce the number of draw operations performed each frame.

In versions prior to love.graphics.isSupported("canvas") could be used to check for support at runtime.

entries.name: generateMipmaps
entries.description:
Generates mipmaps for the Canvas, based on the contents of the highest-resolution mipmap level.

The Canvas must be created with mipmaps set to a MipmapMode other than 'none' for this function to work. It should only be called while the Canvas is not the active render target.

If the mipmap mode is set to 'auto', this function is automatically called inside love.graphics.setCanvas when switching from this Canvas to another Canvas or to the main screen.

entries.name: getMipmapMode
entries.description: Gets the MipmapMode this Canvas was created with.

returns.name: mode
returns.description: The mipmap mode this Canvas was created with.

entries.name: getMSAA
entries.description:
Gets the number of multisample antialiasing (MSAA) samples used when drawing to the Canvas.

This may be different than the number used as an argument to love.graphics.newCanvas if the system running LÖVE doesn't support that number.

returns.name: samples
returns.description: The number of multisample antialiasing samples used by the canvas when drawing to it.

entries.name: newImageData
entries.description: Generates ImageData from the contents of the Canvas.

returns.name: data
returns.description: The new ImageData made from the Canvas' contents.

arguments.name: slice
arguments.description: The cubemap face index, array index, or depth layer for cubemap, array, or volume type Canvases, respectively. This argument is ignored for regular 2D canvases.

arguments.name: mipmap
arguments.description: The mipmap index to use, for Canvases with mipmaps.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-axis of the top-left corner (in pixels) of the area within the Canvas to capture.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-axis of the top-left corner (in pixels) of the area within the Canvas to capture.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width in pixels of the area within the Canvas to capture.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height in pixels of the area within the Canvas to capture.

returns.name: data
returns.description: The new ImageData made from the Canvas' contents.

entries.name: renderTo
entries.description:
Render to the Canvas using a function.

This is a shortcut to love.graphics.setCanvas:

canvas:renderTo( func )

is the same as

love.graphics.setCanvas( canvas )

func()

love.graphics.setCanvas()

arguments.name: func
arguments.description: A function performing drawing operations.
Additional arguments to call the function with.

entries.name: ChainShape
entries.description:
A ChainShape consists of multiple line segments. It can be used to create the boundaries of your terrain. The shape does not have volume and can only collide with PolygonShape and CircleShape.

Unlike the PolygonShape, the ChainShape does not have a vertices limit or has to form a convex shape, but self intersections are not supported.

entries.name: getChildEdge
entries.description: Returns a child of the shape as an EdgeShape.

arguments.name: index
arguments.description: The index of the child.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The child as an EdgeShape.

entries.name: getNextVertex
entries.description:
Gets the vertex that establishes a connection to the next shape.

Setting next and previous ChainShape vertices can help prevent unwanted collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the vertex, or nil if ChainShape:setNextVertex hasn't been called.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the vertex, or nil if ChainShape:setNextVertex hasn't been called.

entries.name: getPoint
entries.description: Returns a point of the shape.

arguments.name: index
arguments.description: The index of the point to return.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the point.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the point.

entries.name: getPoints
entries.description: Returns all points of the shape.

returns.name: x1
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the first point.

returns.name: y1
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the first point.

returns.name: x2
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the second point.

returns.name: y2
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the second point.

entries.name: getPreviousVertex
entries.description:
Gets the vertex that establishes a connection to the previous shape.

Setting next and previous ChainShape vertices can help prevent unwanted collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the vertex, or nil if ChainShape:setPreviousVertex hasn't been called.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the vertex, or nil if ChainShape:setPreviousVertex hasn't been called.

entries.name: getVertexCount
entries.description: Returns the number of vertices the shape has.

returns.name: count
returns.description: The number of vertices.

entries.name: setNextVertex
entries.description:
Sets a vertex that establishes a connection to the next shape.

This can help prevent unwanted collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-component of the vertex.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-component of the vertex.

entries.name: setPreviousVertex
entries.description:
Sets a vertex that establishes a connection to the previous shape.

This can help prevent unwanted collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-component of the vertex.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-component of the vertex.

entries.name: Channel
entries.description: An object which can be used to send and receive data between different threads.

entries.name: clear
entries.description: Clears all the messages in the Channel queue.

entries.name: demand
entries.description:
Retrieves the value of a Channel message and removes it from the message queue.

It waits until a message is in the queue then returns the message value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The contents of the message.

arguments.name: timeout
arguments.description: The maximum amount of time to wait.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The contents of the message or nil if the timeout expired.

entries.name: getCount
entries.description: Retrieves the number of messages in the thread Channel queue.

returns.name: count
returns.description: The number of messages in the queue.

entries.name: hasRead
entries.description: Gets whether a pushed value has been popped or otherwise removed from the Channel.

arguments.name: id
arguments.description: An id value previously returned by Channel:push.

returns.name: hasread
returns.description: Whether the value represented by the id has been removed from the Channel via Channel:pop, Channel:demand, or Channel:clear.

entries.name: peek
entries.description:
Retrieves the value of a Channel message, but leaves it in the queue.

It returns nil if there's no message in the queue.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The contents of the message.

entries.name: performAtomic
entries.description:
Executes the specified function atomically with respect to this Channel.

Calling multiple methods in a row on the same Channel is often useful. However if multiple Threads are calling this Channel's methods at the same time, the different calls on each Thread might end up interleaved (e.g. one or more of the second thread's calls may happen in between the first thread's calls.)

This method avoids that issue by making sure the Thread calling the method has exclusive access to the Channel until the specified function has returned.

arguments.name: func
arguments.description: The function to call, the form of function(channel, arg1, arg2, ...) end. The Channel is passed as the first argument to the function when it is called.
Additional arguments that the given function will receive when it is called.

returns.name: ret1
returns.description: The first return value of the given function (if any.)
Any other return values.

entries.name: pop
entries.description:
Retrieves the value of a Channel message and removes it from the message queue.

It returns nil if there are no messages in the queue.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The contents of the message.

entries.name: push
entries.description:
Send a message to the thread Channel.

See Variant for the list of supported types.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The contents of the message.

returns.name: id
returns.description: Identifier which can be supplied to Channel:hasRead

entries.name: supply
entries.description:
Send a message to the thread Channel and wait for a thread to accept it.

See Variant for the list of supported types.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The contents of the message.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the message was successfully supplied (always true).

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The contents of the message.

arguments.name: timeout
arguments.description: The maximum amount of time to wait.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the message was successfully supplied before the timeout expired.

entries.name: CircleShape
entries.description: Circle extends Shape and adds a radius and a local position.

entries.name: getPoint
entries.description: Gets the center point of the circle shape.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the center point of the circle.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the center point of the circle.

entries.name: getRadius
entries.description: Gets the radius of the circle shape.

returns.name: radius
returns.description: The radius of the circle

entries.name: setPoint
entries.description: Sets the location of the center of the circle shape.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-component of the new center point of the circle.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-component of the new center point of the circle.

entries.name: setRadius
entries.description: Sets the radius of the circle.

arguments.name: radius
arguments.description: The radius of the circle

entries.name: CompareMode
entries.description: Different types of per-pixel stencil test and depth test comparisons. The pixels of an object will be drawn if the comparison succeeds, for each pixel that the object touches.

constants.name: equal
constants.description:
* stencil tests: the stencil value of the pixel must be equal to the supplied value.
* depth tests: the depth value of the drawn object at that pixel must be equal to the existing depth value of that pixel.

constants.name: notequal
constants.description:
* stencil tests: the stencil value of the pixel must not be equal to the supplied value.
* depth tests: the depth value of the drawn object at that pixel must not be equal to the existing depth value of that pixel.

constants.name: less
constants.description:
* stencil tests: the stencil value of the pixel must be less than the supplied value.
* depth tests: the depth value of the drawn object at that pixel must be less than the existing depth value of that pixel.

constants.name: lequal
constants.description:
* stencil tests: the stencil value of the pixel must be less than or equal to the supplied value.
* depth tests: the depth value of the drawn object at that pixel must be less than or equal to the existing depth value of that pixel.

constants.name: gequal
constants.description:
* stencil tests: the stencil value of the pixel must be greater than or equal to the supplied value.
* depth tests: the depth value of the drawn object at that pixel must be greater than or equal to the existing depth value of that pixel.

constants.name: greater
constants.description:
* stencil tests: the stencil value of the pixel must be greater than the supplied value.
* depth tests: the depth value of the drawn object at that pixel must be greater than the existing depth value of that pixel.

constants.name: never
constants.description: Objects will never be drawn.

constants.name: always
constants.description: Objects will always be drawn. Effectively disables the depth or stencil test.

entries.name: CompressedData
entries.description:
Represents byte data compressed using a specific algorithm.

love.data.decompress can be used to de-compress the data (or love.math.decompress in 0.10.2 or earlier).

entries.name: getFormat
entries.description: Gets the compression format of the CompressedData.

returns.name: format
returns.description: The format of the CompressedData.

entries.name: CompressedDataFormat
entries.description: Compressed data formats.

constants.name: lz4
constants.description: The LZ4 compression format. Compresses and decompresses very quickly, but the compression ratio is not the best. LZ4-HC is used when compression level 9 is specified. Some benchmarks are available here.

constants.name: zlib
constants.description: The zlib format is DEFLATE-compressed data with a small bit of header data. Compresses relatively slowly and decompresses moderately quickly, and has a decent compression ratio.

constants.name: gzip
constants.description: The gzip format is DEFLATE-compressed data with a slightly larger header than zlib. Since it uses DEFLATE it has the same compression characteristics as the zlib format.

constants.name: deflate
constants.description: Raw DEFLATE-compressed data (no header).

entries.name: CompressedImageData
entries.description:
Represents compressed image data designed to stay compressed in RAM.

CompressedImageData encompasses standard compressed texture formats such as  DXT1, DXT5, and BC5 / 3Dc.

You can't draw CompressedImageData directly to the screen. See Image for that.

entries.name: getDimensions
entries.description: Gets the width and height of the CompressedImageData.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the CompressedImageData.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the CompressedImageData.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: A mipmap level. Must be in the range of CompressedImageData:getMipmapCount().

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of a specific mipmap level of the CompressedImageData.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of a specific mipmap level of the CompressedImageData.

entries.name: getFormat
entries.description: Gets the format of the CompressedImageData.

returns.name: format
returns.description: The format of the CompressedImageData.

entries.name: getHeight
entries.description: Gets the height of the CompressedImageData.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the CompressedImageData.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: A mipmap level. Must be in the range of CompressedImageData:getMipmapCount().

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of a specific mipmap level of the CompressedImageData.

entries.name: getMipmapCount
entries.description: Gets the number of mipmap levels in the CompressedImageData. The base mipmap level (original image) is included in the count.

variants.description: Mipmap filtering cannot be activated for an Image:setMipmapFilter will error. Most tools which can create compressed textures are able to automatically generate mipmaps for them in the same file.

returns.name: mipmaps
returns.description: The number of mipmap levels stored in the CompressedImageData.

entries.name: getWidth
entries.description: Gets the width of the CompressedImageData.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the CompressedImageData.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: A mipmap level. Must be in the range of  CompressedImageData:getMipmapCount().

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of a specific mipmap level of the CompressedImageData.

entries.name: CompressedImageFormat
entries.description:
Compressed image data formats. Here and here are a couple overviews of many of the formats.

Unlike traditional PNG or jpeg, these formats stay compressed in RAM and in the graphics card's VRAM. This is good for saving memory space as well as improving performance, since the graphics card will be able to keep more of the image's pixels in its fast-access cache when drawing it.

constants.name: DXT1
constants.description: The DXT1 format. RGB data at 4 bits per pixel (compared to 32 bits for ImageData and regular Images.) Suitable for fully opaque images on desktop systems.

constants.name: DXT3
constants.description: The DXT3 format. RGBA data at 8 bits per pixel. Smooth variations in opacity do not mix well with this format.

constants.name: DXT5
constants.description: The DXT5 format. RGBA data at 8 bits per pixel. Recommended for images with varying opacity on desktop systems.

constants.name: BC4
constants.description: The BC4 format (also known as 3Dc+ or ATI1.) Stores just the red channel, at 4 bits per pixel.

constants.name: BC4s
constants.description: The signed variant of the BC4 format. Same as above but pixel values in the texture are in the range of 1 instead of 1 in shaders.

constants.name: BC5
constants.description: The BC5 format (also known as 3Dc or ATI2.) Stores red and green channels at 8 bits per pixel.

constants.name: BC5s
constants.description: The signed variant of the BC5 format.

constants.name: BC6h
constants.description: The BC6H format. Stores half-precision floating-point RGB data in the range of 65504 at 8 bits per pixel. Suitable for HDR images on desktop systems.

constants.name: BC6hs
constants.description: The signed variant of the BC6H format. Stores RGB data in the range of +65504.

constants.name: BC7
constants.description: The BC7 format (also known as BPTC.) Stores RGB or RGBA data at 8 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ETC1
constants.description: The ETC1 format. RGB data at 4 bits per pixel. Suitable for fully opaque images on older Android devices.

constants.name: ETC2rgb
constants.description: The RGB variant of the ETC2 format. RGB data at 4 bits per pixel. Suitable for fully opaque images on newer mobile devices.

constants.name: ETC2rgba
constants.description: The RGBA variant of the ETC2 format. RGBA data at 8 bits per pixel. Recommended for images with varying opacity on newer mobile devices.

constants.name: ETC2rgba1
constants.description: The RGBA variant of the ETC2 format where pixels are either fully transparent or fully opaque. RGBA data at 4 bits per pixel.

constants.name: EACr
constants.description: The single-channel variant of the EAC format. Stores just the red channel, at 4 bits per pixel.

constants.name: EACrs
constants.description: The signed single-channel variant of the EAC format. Same as above but pixel values in the texture are in the range of 1 instead of 1 in shaders.

constants.name: EACrg
constants.description: The two-channel variant of the EAC format. Stores red and green channels at 8 bits per pixel.

constants.name: EACrgs
constants.description: The signed two-channel variant of the EAC format.

constants.name: PVR1rgb2
constants.description: The 2 bit per pixel RGB variant of the PVRTC1 format. Stores RGB data at 2 bits per pixel. Textures compressed with PVRTC1 formats must be square and power-of-two sized.

constants.name: PVR1rgb4
constants.description: The 4 bit per pixel RGB variant of the PVRTC1 format. Stores RGB data at 4 bits per pixel.

constants.name: PVR1rgba2
constants.description: The 2 bit per pixel RGBA variant of the PVRTC1 format.

constants.name: PVR1rgba4
constants.description: The 4 bit per pixel RGBA variant of the PVRTC1 format.

constants.name: ASTC4x4
constants.description: The 4x4 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 8 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC5x4
constants.description: The 5x4 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 6.4 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC5x5
constants.description: The 5x5 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 5.12 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC6x5
constants.description: The 6x5 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 4.27 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC6x6
constants.description: The 6x6 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 3.56 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC8x5
constants.description: The 8x5 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 3.2 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC8x6
constants.description: The 8x6 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 2.67 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC8x8
constants.description: The 8x8 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 2 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC10x5
constants.description: The 10x5 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 2.56 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC10x6
constants.description: The 10x6 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 2.13 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC10x8
constants.description: The 10x8 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 1.6 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC10x10
constants.description: The 10x10 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 1.28 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC12x10
constants.description: The 12x10 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 1.07 bits per pixel.

constants.name: ASTC12x12
constants.description: The 12x12 pixels per block variant of the ASTC format. RGBA data at 0.89 bits per pixel.

entries.name: Contact
entries.description: Contacts are objects created to manage collisions in worlds.

entries.name: getChildren
entries.description:
Gets the child indices of the shapes of the two colliding fixtures. For ChainShapes, an index of 1 is the first edge in the chain.
Used together with Fixture:rayCast or ChainShape:getChildEdge.

returns.name: indexA
returns.description: The child index of the first fixture's shape.

returns.name: indexB
returns.description: The child index of the second fixture's shape.

entries.name: getFixtures
entries.description: Gets the two Fixtures that hold the shapes that are in contact.

returns.name: fixtureA
returns.description: The first Fixture.

returns.name: fixtureB
returns.description: The second Fixture.

entries.name: getFriction
entries.description: Get the friction between two shapes that are in contact.

returns.name: friction
returns.description: The friction of the contact.

entries.name: getNormal
entries.description:
Get the normal vector between two shapes that are in contact.

This function returns the coordinates of a unit vector that points from the first shape to the second.

returns.name: nx
returns.description: The x component of the normal vector.

returns.name: ny
returns.description: The y component of the normal vector.

entries.name: getPositions
entries.description: Returns the contact points of the two colliding fixtures. There can be one or two points.

returns.name: x1
returns.description: The x coordinate of the first contact point.

returns.name: y1
returns.description: The y coordinate of the first contact point.

returns.name: x2
returns.description: The x coordinate of the second contact point.

returns.name: y2
returns.description: The y coordinate of the second contact point.

entries.name: getRestitution
entries.description: Get the restitution between two shapes that are in contact.

returns.name: restitution
returns.description: The restitution between the two shapes.

entries.name: isEnabled
entries.description: Returns whether the contact is enabled. The collision will be ignored if a contact gets disabled in the preSolve callback.

returns.name: enabled
returns.description: True if enabled, false otherwise.

entries.name: isTouching
entries.description: Returns whether the two colliding fixtures are touching each other.

returns.name: touching
returns.description: True if they touch or false if not.

entries.name: resetFriction
entries.description: Resets the contact friction to the mixture value of both fixtures.

entries.name: resetRestitution
entries.description: Resets the contact restitution to the mixture value of both fixtures.

entries.name: setEnabled
entries.description: Enables or disables the contact.

arguments.name: enabled
arguments.description: True to enable or false to disable.

entries.name: setFriction
entries.description: Sets the contact friction.

arguments.name: friction
arguments.description: The contact friction.

entries.name: setRestitution
entries.description: Sets the contact restitution.

arguments.name: restitution
arguments.description: The contact restitution.

entries.name: ContainerType
entries.description: Return type of various data-returning functions.

constants.name: data
constants.description: Return type is ByteData.

constants.name: string
constants.description: Return type is string.

entries.name: CullMode
entries.description: How Mesh geometry is culled when rendering.

constants.name: back
constants.description: Back-facing triangles in Meshes are culled (not rendered). The vertex order of a triangle determines whether it is back- or front-facing.

constants.name: front
constants.description: Front-facing triangles in Meshes are culled.

constants.name: none
constants.description: Both back- and front-facing triangles in Meshes are rendered.

entries.name: Cursor
entries.description: Represents a hardware cursor.

entries.name: getType
entries.description: Gets the type of the Cursor.

returns.name: ctype
returns.description: The type of the Cursor.

entries.name: CursorType
entries.description: Types of hardware cursors.

constants.name: image
constants.description: The cursor is using a custom image.

constants.name: arrow
constants.description: An arrow pointer.

constants.name: ibeam
constants.description: An I-beam, normally used when mousing over editable or selectable text.

constants.name: wait
constants.description: Wait graphic.

constants.name: waitarrow
constants.description: Small wait cursor with an arrow pointer.

constants.name: crosshair
constants.description: Crosshair symbol.

constants.name: sizenwse
constants.description: Double arrow pointing to the top-left and bottom-right.

constants.name: sizenesw
constants.description: Double arrow pointing to the top-right and bottom-left.

constants.name: sizewe
constants.description: Double arrow pointing left and right.

constants.name: sizens
constants.description: Double arrow pointing up and down.

constants.name: sizeall
constants.description: Four-pointed arrow pointing up, down, left, and right.

constants.name: no
constants.description: Slashed circle or crossbones.

constants.name: hand
constants.description: Hand symbol.

entries.name: Data
entries.description: The superclass of all data.

entries.name: EdgeShape
entries.description: A EdgeShape is a line segment. They can be used to create the boundaries of your terrain. The shape does not have volume and can only collide with PolygonShape and CircleShape.

entries.name: getNextVertex
entries.description:
Gets the vertex that establishes a connection to the next shape.

Setting next and previous EdgeShape vertices can help prevent unwanted collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the vertex, or nil if EdgeShape:setNextVertex hasn't been called.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the vertex, or nil if EdgeShape:setNextVertex hasn't been called.

returns.name: y1

returns.name: x2
returns.description: The x-component of the second vertex.

returns.name: y2
returns.description: The y-component of the second vertex.

entries.name: getPreviousVertex
entries.description:
Gets the vertex that establishes a connection to the previous shape.

Setting next and previous EdgeShape vertices can help prevent unwanted collisions when a flat shape slides along the edge and moves over to the new shape.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the vertex, or nil if EdgeShape:setPreviousVertex hasn't been called.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the vertex, or nil if EdgeShape:setPreviousVertex hasn't been called.

entries.name: EffectType
entries.description: The different types of effects supported by love.audio.setEffect.

constants.name: chorus
constants.description: Plays multiple copies of the sound with slight pitch and time variation. Used to make sounds sound "fuller" or "thicker".

constants.name: compressor
constants.description: Decreases the dynamic range of the sound, making the loud and quiet parts closer in volume, producing a more uniform amplitude throughout time.

constants.name: distortion
constants.description: Alters the sound by amplifying it until it clips, shearing off parts of the signal, leading to a compressed and distorted sound.

constants.name: echo
constants.description: Decaying feedback based effect, on the order of seconds. Also known as delay; causes the sound to repeat at regular intervals at a decreasing volume.

constants.name: equalizer
constants.description: Adjust the frequency components of the sound using a 4-band (low-shelf, two band-pass and a high-shelf) equalizer.

constants.name: flanger
constants.description: Plays two copies of the sound; while varying the phase, or equivalently delaying one of them, by amounts on the order of milliseconds, resulting in phasing sounds.

constants.name: reverb
constants.description: Decaying feedback based effect, on the order of milliseconds. Used to simulate the reflection off of the surroundings.

constants.name: ringmodulator
constants.description: An implementation of amplitude modulation; multiplies the source signal with a simple waveform, to produce either volume changes, or inharmonic overtones.

entries.name: EffectWaveform
entries.description: The different types of waveforms that can be used with the '''ringmodulator''' EffectType.

constants.name: sawtooth
constants.description: A sawtooth wave, also known as a ramp wave. Named for its linear rise, and (near-)instantaneous fall along time.

constants.name: sine
constants.description: A sine wave. Follows a trigonometric sine function.

constants.name: square
constants.description: A square wave. Switches between high and low states (near-)instantaneously.

constants.name: triangle
constants.description: A triangle wave. Follows a linear rise and fall that repeats periodically.

entries.name: setBuffer
entries.description:
Sets the buffer mode for a file opened for writing or appending. Files with buffering enabled will not write data to the disk until the buffer size limit is reached, depending on the buffer mode.

File:flush will force any buffered data to be written to the disk.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: The buffer mode to use.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: The maximum size in bytes of the file's buffer.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the buffer mode was successfully set.

returns.name: errorstr
returns.description: The error string, if the buffer mode could not be set and an error occurred.

entries.name: tell
entries.description: Returns the position in the file.

entries.name: write
entries.description: Write data to a file.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The string data to write.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: How many bytes to write.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the operation was successful.

returns.name: err
returns.description: The error string if an error occurred.

variants.description:
'''Writing to multiple lines''': In Windows, some text editors (e.g. Notepad before Windows 10 1809) only treat CRLF ('\r\n') as a new line.

--example

f = love.filesystem.newFile('note.txt')

f:open('w')

for i = 1, 10 do

    f:write('This is line '..i..'!\r\n')

end

f:close()

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The Data object to write.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: How many bytes to write.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the operation was successful.

returns.name: errorstr
returns.description: The error string if an error occurred.

Categories and masks are encoded as the bits of a 16-bit integer.
