returns.description: If the fixture is a sensor.

entries.name: rayCast
entries.description:
Casts a ray against the shape of the fixture and returns the surface normal vector and the line position where the ray hit. If the ray missed the shape, nil will be returned.

The ray starts on the first point of the input line and goes towards the second point of the line. The fifth argument is the maximum distance the ray is going to travel as a scale factor of the input line length.

The childIndex parameter is used to specify which child of a parent shape, such as a ChainShape, will be ray casted. For ChainShapes, the index of 1 is the first edge on the chain. Ray casting a parent shape will only test the child specified so if you want to test every shape of the parent, you must loop through all of its children.

The world position of the impact can be calculated by multiplying the line vector with the third return value and adding it to the line starting point.

hitx, hity = x1 + (x2 - x1) * fraction, y1 + (y2 - y1) * fraction

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the input line starting point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the input line starting point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the input line end point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the input line end point.

arguments.name: maxFraction
arguments.description: Ray length parameter.

arguments.name: childIndex
arguments.description: The index of the child the ray gets cast against.

returns.name: xn
returns.description: The x component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit the shape.

returns.name: yn
returns.description: The y component of the normal vector of the edge where the ray hit the shape.

returns.name: fraction
returns.description: The position on the input line where the intersection happened as a factor of the line length.

entries.name: setCategory
entries.description:
Sets the categories the fixture belongs to. There can be up to 16 categories represented as a number from 1 to 16.

All fixture's default category is 1.

arguments.description: The categories.

entries.name: setDensity
entries.description: Sets the density of the fixture. Call Body:resetMassData if this needs to take effect immediately.

arguments.name: density
arguments.description: The fixture density in kilograms per square meter.

entries.name: setFilterData
entries.description:
Sets the filter data of the fixture.

Groups, categories, and mask can be used to define the collision behaviour of the fixture.

If two fixtures are in the same group they either always collide if the group is positive, or never collide if it's negative. If the group is zero or they do not match, then the contact filter checks if the fixtures select a category of the other fixture with their masks. The fixtures do not collide if that's not the case. If they do have each other's categories selected, the return value of the custom contact filter will be used. They always collide if none was set.

There can be up to 16 categories. Categories and masks are encoded as the bits of a 16-bit integer.

When created, prior to calling this function, all fixtures have category set to 1, mask set to 65535 (all categories) and group set to 0.

This function allows setting all filter data for a fixture at once. To set only the categories, the mask or the group, you can use Fixture:setCategory, Fixture:setMask or Fixture:setGroupIndex respectively.

arguments.name: categories
arguments.description: The categories as an integer from 0 to 65535.

arguments.name: mask
arguments.description: The mask as an integer from 0 to 65535.

arguments.name: group
arguments.description: The group as an integer from -32768 to 32767.

entries.name: setFriction
entries.description:
Sets the friction of the fixture.

Friction determines how shapes react when they 'slide' along other shapes. Low friction indicates a slippery surface, like ice, while high friction indicates a rough surface, like concrete. Range: 0.0 - 1.0.

arguments.name: friction
arguments.description: The fixture friction.

entries.name: setGroupIndex
entries.description:
Sets the group the fixture belongs to. Fixtures with the same group will always collide if the group is positive or never collide if it's negative. The group zero means no group.

The groups range from -32768 to 32767.

arguments.name: group
arguments.description: The group as an integer from -32768 to 32767.

entries.name: setMask
entries.description:
Sets the category mask of the fixture. There can be up to 16 categories represented as a number from 1 to 16.

This fixture will '''NOT''' collide with the fixtures that are in the selected categories if the other fixture also has a category of this fixture selected.

arguments.description: The masks.

entries.name: setRestitution
entries.description: Sets the restitution of the fixture.

arguments.name: restitution
arguments.description: The fixture restitution.

entries.name: setSensor
entries.description:
Sets whether the fixture should act as a sensor.

Sensors do not cause collision responses, but the begin-contact and end-contact World callbacks will still be called for this fixture.

arguments.name: sensor
arguments.description: The sensor status.

entries.name: setUserData
entries.description:
Associates a Lua value with the fixture.

To delete the reference, explicitly pass nil.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The Lua value to associate with the fixture.

entries.name: testPoint
entries.description: Checks if a point is inside the shape of the fixture.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the point.

returns.name: isInside
returns.description: True if the point is inside or false if it is outside.

entries.name: Font
entries.description: Defines the shape of characters that can be drawn onto the screen.

entries.name: getAscent
entries.description:
Gets the ascent of the Font.

The ascent spans the distance between the baseline and the top of the glyph that reaches farthest from the baseline.

returns.name: ascent
returns.description: The ascent of the Font in pixels.

entries.name: getBaseline
entries.description:
Gets the baseline of the Font.

Most scripts share the notion of a baseline: an imaginary horizontal line on which characters rest. In some scripts, parts of glyphs lie below the baseline.

returns.name: baseline
returns.description: The baseline of the Font in pixels.

entries.name: getDescent
entries.description:
Gets the descent of the Font.

The descent spans the distance between the baseline and the lowest descending glyph in a typeface.

returns.name: descent
returns.description: The descent of the Font in pixels.

entries.name: getDPIScale
entries.description:
Gets the DPI scale factor of the Font.

The DPI scale factor represents relative pixel density. A DPI scale factor of 2 means the font's glyphs have twice the pixel density in each dimension (4 times as many pixels in the same area) compared to a font with a DPI scale factor of 1.

The font size of TrueType fonts is scaled internally by the font's specified DPI scale factor. By default, LÖVE uses the screen's DPI scale factor when creating TrueType fonts.

returns.name: dpiscale
returns.description: The DPI scale factor of the Font.

entries.name: getFilter
entries.description: Gets the filter mode for a font.

returns.name: min
returns.description: Filter mode used when minifying the font.

returns.name: mag
returns.description: Filter mode used when magnifying the font.

returns.name: anisotropy
returns.description: Maximum amount of anisotropic filtering used.

entries.name: getHeight
entries.description:
Gets the height of the Font.

The height of the font is the size including any spacing; the height which it will need.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the Font in pixels.

entries.name: getKerning
entries.description:
Gets the kerning between two characters in the Font.

Kerning is normally handled automatically in love.graphics.print, Text objects, Font:getWidth, Font:getWrap, etc. This function is useful when stitching text together manually.

arguments.name: leftchar
arguments.description: The left character.

arguments.name: rightchar
arguments.description: The right character.

returns.name: kerning
returns.description: The kerning amount to add to the spacing between the two characters. May be negative.

arguments.name: leftglyph
arguments.description: The unicode number for the left glyph.

arguments.name: rightglyph
arguments.description: The unicode number for the right glyph.

returns.name: kerning
returns.description: The kerning amount to add to the spacing between the two characters. May be negative.

entries.name: getLineHeight
entries.description:
Gets the line height.

This will be the value previously set by Font:setLineHeight, or 1.0 by default.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The current line height.

entries.name: getWidth
entries.description: Determines the maximum width (accounting for newlines) taken by the given string.

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: A string.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the text.

entries.name: getWrap
entries.description:
Gets formatting information for text, given a wrap limit.

This function accounts for newlines correctly (i.e. '\n').

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: The text that will be wrapped.

arguments.name: wraplimit
arguments.description: The maximum width in pixels of each line that ''text'' is allowed before wrapping.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The maximum width of the wrapped text.

returns.name: wrappedtext
returns.description: A sequence containing each line of text that was wrapped.

arguments.name: coloredtext
arguments.description: A table containing colors and strings to add to the object, in the form of {color1, string1, color2, string2, ...}.

table.name: color1
table.description: A table containing red, green, blue, and optional alpha components to use as a color for the next string in the table, in the form of {red, green, blue, alpha}.

table.name: string1
table.description: A string of text which has a color specified by the previous color.

table.name: color2
table.description: A table containing red, green, blue, and optional alpha components to use as a color for the next string in the table, in the form of {red, green, blue, alpha}.

table.name: string2
table.description: A string of text which has a color specified by the previous color.
Additional colors and strings.

arguments.name: wraplimit
arguments.description: The maximum width in pixels of each line that ''text'' is allowed before wrapping.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The maximum width of the wrapped text.

returns.name: wrappedtext
returns.description: A sequence containing each line of text that was wrapped.

entries.name: hasGlyphs
entries.description: Gets whether the Font can render a character or string.

arguments.name: text
arguments.description: A UTF-8 encoded unicode string.

returns.name: hasglyph
returns.description: Whether the font can render all the UTF-8 characters in the string.

arguments.name: character1
arguments.description: A unicode character.

arguments.name: character2
arguments.description: Another unicode character.

returns.name: hasglyph
returns.description: Whether the font can render all the glyphs represented by the characters.

arguments.name: codepoint1
arguments.description: A unicode codepoint number.

arguments.name: codepoint2
arguments.description: Another unicode codepoint number.

returns.name: hasglyph
returns.description: Whether the font can render all the glyphs represented by the codepoint numbers.

entries.name: setFallbacks
entries.description: Sets the fallback fonts. When the Font doesn't contain a glyph, it will substitute the glyph from the next subsequent fallback Fonts. This is akin to setting a 'font stack' in Cascading Style Sheets (CSS).

variants.description:
If this is called it should be before love.graphics.print, Font:getWrap, and other Font methods which use glyph positioning information are called.

Every fallback Font must be created from the same file type as the primary Font. For example, a Font created from a .ttf file can only use fallback Fonts that were created from .ttf files.

arguments.name: fallbackfont1
arguments.description: The first fallback Font to use.
Additional fallback Fonts.

entries.description: Sets the filter mode for a font.

arguments.name: min

arguments.name: mag
arguments.description: How to scale a font up.

When rendering the font in lines the actual height will be determined by the line height multiplied by the height of the font. The default is 1.0.

entries.name: HintingMode
entries.description: True Type hinting mode.

constants.name: normal
constants.description: Default hinting. Should be preferred for typical antialiased fonts.

constants.name: light
constants.description: Results in fuzzier text but can sometimes preserve the original glyph shapes of the text better than normal hinting.

constants.name: mono
constants.description: Results in aliased / unsmoothed text with either full opacity or completely transparent pixels. Should be used when antialiasing is not desired for the font.

constants.name: none
constants.description: Disables hinting for the font. Results in fuzzier text.

entries.name: Image
entries.description: Drawable image type.

entries.name: isCompressed
entries.description:
Gets whether the Image was created from CompressedData.

Compressed images take up less space in VRAM, and drawing a compressed image will generally be more efficient than drawing one created from raw pixel data.

returns.name: compressed
returns.description: Whether the Image is stored as a compressed texture on the GPU.

entries.name: isFormatLinear
entries.description:
Gets whether the Image was created with the linear (non-gamma corrected) flag set to true.

This method always returns false when gamma-correct rendering is not enabled.

returns.name: linear
returns.description: Whether the Image's internal pixel format is linear (not gamma corrected), when gamma-correct rendering is enabled.

entries.name: replacePixels
entries.description: Replace the contents of an Image.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The new ImageData to replace the contents with.

arguments.name: slice
arguments.description: Which cubemap face, array index, or volume layer to replace, if applicable.

arguments.name: mipmap
arguments.description: The mimap level to replace, if the Image has mipmaps.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-offset in pixels from the top-left of the image to replace. The given ImageData's width plus this value must not be greater than the pixel width of the Image's specified mipmap level.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-offset in pixels from the top-left of the image to replace. The given ImageData's height plus this value must not be greater than the pixel height of the Image's specified mipmap level.

arguments.name: reloadmipmaps
arguments.description: Whether to generate new mipmaps after replacing the Image's pixels. True by default if the Image was created with automatically generated mipmaps, false by default otherwise.

entries.name: ImageData
entries.description:
Raw (decoded) image data.

You can't draw ImageData directly to screen. See Image for that.

entries.description: Encodes the ImageData and optionally writes it to the save directory.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format to encode the image as.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filename to write the file to. If nil, no file will be written but the FileData will still be returned.

returns.name: filedata
returns.description: The encoded image as a new FileData object.

arguments.name: outFile
arguments.description: Name of a file to write encoded data to. The format will be automatically deduced from the file extension.

arguments.name: outFile

arguments.name: format

entries.name: getPixel
entries.description:
Gets the color of a pixel at a specific position in the image.

Valid x and y values start at 0 and go up to image width and height minus 1. Non-integer values are floored.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The position of the pixel on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The position of the pixel on the y-axis.

returns.name: r
returns.description: The red component (0-1).

returns.name: g
returns.description: The green component (0-1).

returns.name: b
returns.description: The blue component (0-1).

returns.name: a
returns.description: The alpha component (0-1).

entries.name: getWidth
entries.description: Gets the width of the ImageData in pixels.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the ImageData in pixels.

entries.name: mapPixel
entries.description:
Transform an image by applying a function to every pixel.

This function is a higher-order function. It takes another function as a parameter, and calls it once for each pixel in the ImageData.

The passed function is called with six parameters for each pixel in turn. The parameters are numbers that represent the x and y coordinates of the pixel and its red, green, blue and alpha values. The function should return the new red, green, blue, and alpha values for that pixel.

function pixelFunction(x, y, r, g, b, a)

    -- template for defining your own pixel mapping function

    -- perform computations giving the new values for r, g, b and a

    -- ...

    return r, g, b, a

end

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: pixelFunction
arguments.description: Function to apply to every pixel.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-axis of the top-left corner of the area within the ImageData to apply the function to.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-axis of the top-left corner of the area within the ImageData to apply the function to.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the area within the ImageData to apply the function to.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height of the area within the ImageData to apply the function to.

entries.name: paste
entries.description: Paste into ImageData from another source ImageData.

variants.description: Note that this function just replaces the contents in the destination rectangle; it does not do any alpha blending.

arguments.name: source
arguments.description: Source ImageData from which to copy.

arguments.name: dx
arguments.description: Destination top-left position on x-axis.

arguments.name: dy
arguments.description: Destination top-left position on y-axis.

arguments.name: sx
arguments.description: Source top-left position on x-axis.

arguments.name: sy
arguments.description: Source top-left position on y-axis.

arguments.name: sw
arguments.description: Source width.

arguments.name: sh
arguments.description: Source height.

Sets the color of a pixel at a specific position in the image.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

entries.name: getDeviceInfo
entries.description:
Gets the USB vendor ID, product ID, and product version numbers of joystick which consistent across operating systems.

Can be used to show different icons, etc. for different gamepads.

variants.description: Some Linux distribution may not ship with SDL 2.0.6 or later, in which case this function will returns 0 for all the three values.

returns.description: The USB vendor ID of the joystick.

returns.name: productID

returns.name: productVersion

entries.description: Gets the direction of a virtual gamepad axis. If the Joystick isn't recognized as a gamepad or isn't connected, this function will always return 0.

arguments.name: axis
arguments.description: The virtual axis to be checked.

returns.name: direction
returns.description: Current value of the axis.

entries.name: getGamepadMapping
entries.description: Gets the button, axis or hat that a virtual gamepad input is bound to.

variants.description: Returns nil if the Joystick isn't recognized as a gamepad or the virtual gamepad axis is not bound to a Joystick input.

arguments.name: axis
arguments.description: The virtual gamepad axis to get the binding for.

returns.name: inputtype
returns.description: The type of input the virtual gamepad axis is bound to.

returns.name: inputindex
returns.description: The index of the Joystick's button, axis or hat that the virtual gamepad axis is bound to.

returns.name: hatdirection
returns.description: The direction of the hat, if the virtual gamepad axis is bound to a hat. nil otherwise.

variants.description: The physical locations for the virtual gamepad axes and buttons correspond as closely as possible to the layout of a standard Xbox 360 controller.

arguments.name: button
arguments.description: The virtual gamepad button to get the binding for.

returns.name: inputtype
returns.description: The type of input the virtual gamepad button is bound to.

returns.name: inputindex
returns.description: The index of the Joystick's button, axis or hat that the virtual gamepad button is bound to.

returns.name: hatdirection
returns.description: The direction of the hat, if the virtual gamepad button is bound to a hat. nil otherwise.

entries.name: getGamepadMappingString
entries.description:
Gets the full gamepad mapping string of this Joystick, or nil if it's not recognized as a gamepad.

The mapping string contains binding information used to map the Joystick's buttons an axes to the standard gamepad layout, and can be used later with love.joystick.loadGamepadMappings.

returns.name: mappingstring
returns.description: A string containing the Joystick's gamepad mappings, or nil if the Joystick is not recognized as a gamepad.

entries.name: getGUID
entries.description: Gets a stable GUID unique to the type of the physical joystick which does not change over time. For example, all Sony Dualshock 3 controllers in OS X have the same GUID. The value is platform-dependent.

returns.name: guid
returns.description: The Joystick type's OS-dependent unique identifier.

entries.name: getHat
entries.description: Gets the direction of the Joystick's hat.

arguments.name: hat
arguments.description: The index of the hat to be checked.

returns.name: direction
returns.description: The direction the hat is pushed.

entries.name: getHatCount
entries.description: Gets the number of hats on the joystick.

returns.name: hats
returns.description: How many hats the joystick has.

entries.name: getID
entries.description: Gets the joystick's unique identifier. The identifier will remain the same for the life of the game, even when the Joystick is disconnected and reconnected, but it '''will''' change when the game is re-launched.

returns.name: id
returns.description: The Joystick's unique identifier. Remains the same as long as the game is running.

returns.name: instanceid
returns.description: Unique instance identifier. Changes every time the Joystick is reconnected. nil if the Joystick is not connected.

entries.name: getName
entries.description: Gets the name of the joystick.

returns.name: name
returns.description: The name of the joystick.

entries.name: getVibration
entries.description: Gets the current vibration motor strengths on a Joystick with rumble support.

returns.name: left
returns.description: Current strength of the left vibration motor on the Joystick.

returns.name: right
returns.description: Current strength of the right vibration motor on the Joystick.

entries.name: isConnected
entries.description: Gets whether the Joystick is connected.

returns.name: connected
returns.description: True if the Joystick is currently connected, false otherwise.

entries.name: isDown
entries.description:
Checks if a button on the Joystick is pressed.

LÖVE 0.9.0 had a bug which required the button indices passed to Joystick:isDown to be 0-based instead of 1-based, for example button 1 would be 0 for this function. It was fixed in 0.9.1.

arguments.name: buttonN
arguments.description: The index of a button to check.

returns.name: anyDown
returns.description: True if any supplied button is down, false if not.

entries.name: isGamepad
entries.description:
Gets whether the Joystick is recognized as a gamepad. If this is the case, the Joystick's buttons and axes can be used in a standardized manner across different operating systems and joystick models via Joystick:getGamepadAxis, Joystick:isGamepadDown, love.gamepadpressed, and related functions.

LÖVE automatically recognizes most popular controllers with a similar layout to the Xbox 360 controller as gamepads, but you can add more with love.joystick.setGamepadMapping.

variants.description: If the Joystick is recognized as a gamepad, the physical locations for the virtual gamepad axes and buttons correspond as closely as possible to the layout of a standard Xbox 360 controller.

returns.name: isgamepad
returns.description: True if the Joystick is recognized as a gamepad, false otherwise.

entries.name: isGamepadDown
entries.description: Checks if a virtual gamepad button on the Joystick is pressed. If the Joystick is not recognized as a Gamepad or isn't connected, then this function will always return false.

arguments.name: buttonN
arguments.description: The gamepad button to check.

returns.name: anyDown
returns.description: True if any supplied button is down, false if not.

entries.name: isVibrationSupported
entries.description: Gets whether the Joystick supports vibration.

variants.description: The very first call to this function may take more time than expected because SDL's Haptic / Force Feedback subsystem needs to be initialized.

returns.name: supported
returns.description: True if rumble / force feedback vibration is supported on this Joystick, false if not.

entries.name: setVibration
entries.description: Sets the vibration motor speeds on a Joystick with rumble support. Most common gamepads have this functionality, although not all drivers give proper support. Use Joystick:isVibrationSupported to check.

arguments.name: left
arguments.description: Strength of the left vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

arguments.name: right
arguments.description: Strength of the right vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the vibration was successfully applied, false if not.

variants.description: Disables vibration.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the vibration was successfully disabled, false if not.

variants.description:
If the Joystick only has a single vibration motor, it will still work but it will use the largest value of the left and right parameters.

The Xbox 360 controller on Mac OS X only has support for vibration if a modified version of the Tattiebogle driver is used.

The very first call to this function may take more time than expected because SDL's Haptic / Force Feedback subsystem needs to be initialized.

arguments.name: left
arguments.description: Strength of the left vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

arguments.name: right
arguments.description: Strength of the right vibration motor on the Joystick. Must be in the range of 1.

arguments.name: duration
arguments.description: The duration of the vibration in seconds. A negative value means infinite duration.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the vibration was successfully applied, false if not.

entries.name: conf
entries.description: If a file called conf.lua is present in your game folder (or .love file), it is run before the LÖVE modules are loaded. You can use this file to overwrite the love.conf function, which is later called by the LÖVE 'boot' script. Using the love.conf function, you can set some configuration options, and change things like the default size of the window, which modules are loaded, and other stuff.

arguments.name: t
arguments.description:
The love.conf function takes one argument: a table filled with all the default values which you can overwrite to your liking. If you want to change the default window size, for instance, do:

function love.conf(t)
    t.window.width = 1024
    t.window.height = 768
end

If you don't need the physics module or joystick module, do the following.

function love.conf(t)
    t.modules.joystick = false
    t.modules.physics = false
end

Setting unused modules to false is encouraged when you release your game. It reduces startup time slightly (especially if the joystick module is disabled) and reduces memory usage (slightly).

Note that you can't disable love.filesystem; it's mandatory. The same goes for the love module itself. love.graphics needs love.window to be enabled.

table.name: identity
table.description:
This flag determines the name of the save directory for your game. Note that you can only specify the name, not the location where it will be created:
t.identity = "gabe_HL3" -- Correct

t.identity = "c:/Users/gabe/HL3" -- Incorrect
Alternatively love.filesystem.setIdentity can be used to set the save directory outside of the config file.

table.name: appendidentity
table.description: This flag determines if game directory should be searched first then save directory (true) or otherwise (false)

table.name: version
table.description: t.version should be a string, representing the version of LÖVE for which your game was made. It should be formatted as "X.Y.Z" where X is the major release number, Y the minor, and Z the patch level. It allows LÖVE to display a warning if it isn't compatible. Its default is the version of LÖVE running.

table.name: console
table.description: Determines whether a console should be opened alongside the game window (Windows only) or not. Note: On OSX you can get console output by running LÖVE through the terminal.

table.name: accelerometerjoystick
table.description: Sets whether the device accelerometer on iOS and Android should be exposed as a 3-axis Joystick. Disabling the accelerometer when it's not used may reduce CPU usage.

table.name: externalstorage
table.description: Sets whether files are saved in external storage (true) or internal storage (false) on Android.

table.name: gammacorrect
table.description: Determines whether gamma-correct rendering is enabled, when the system supports it.

table.name: audio
table.description: Audio options.

table.name: mic
table.description: Request microphone permission from the user. When user allows it, love.audio.getRecordingDevices will lists recording devices available. Otherwise, love.audio.getRecordingDevices returns empty table and a message is shown to inform user when called.

table.name: mixwithsystem
table.description: Sets whether background audio / music from other apps should play while LÖVE is open. See love.system.hasBackgroundMusic for more details.

table.name: window
table.description:
It is possible to defer window creation until love.window.setMode is first called in your code. To do so, set t.window = nil in love.conf (or t.screen = nil in older versions.) If this is done, LÖVE may crash if any function from love.graphics is called before the first love.window.setMode in your code.

The t.window table was named t.screen in versions prior to 0.9.0. The t.screen table doesn't exist in love.conf in 0.9.0, and the t.window table doesn't exist in love.conf in 0.8.0. This means love.conf will fail to execute (therefore it will fall back to default values) if care is not taken to use the correct table for the LÖVE version being used.

table.name: title
table.description: Sets the title of the window the game is in. Alternatively love.window.setTitle can be used to change the window title outside of the config file.

table.name: icon
table.description: A filepath to an image to use as the window's icon. Not all operating systems support very large icon images. The icon can also be changed with love.window.setIcon.

table.name: width
table.description: Sets the window's dimensions. If these flags are set to 0 LÖVE automatically uses the user's desktop dimensions.

table.name: height
table.description: Sets the window's dimensions. If these flags are set to 0 LÖVE automatically uses the user's desktop dimensions.

table.name: borderless
table.description: Removes all border visuals from the window. Note that the effects may wary between operating systems.

table.name: resizable
table.description: If set to true this allows the user to resize the game's window.

table.name: minwidth
table.description: Sets the minimum width and height for the game's window if it can be resized by the user. If you set lower values to window.width and window.height LÖVE will always favor the minimum dimensions set via window.minwidth and window.minheight.

table.name: minheight
table.description: Sets the minimum width and height for the game's window if it can be resized by the user. If you set lower values to window.width and window.height LÖVE will always favor the minimum dimensions set via window.minwidth and window.minheight.

table.name: fullscreen
table.description: Whether to run the game in fullscreen (true) or windowed (false) mode. The fullscreen can also be toggled via love.window.setFullscreen or love.window.setMode.

table.name: fullscreentype
table.description: Specifies the type of fullscreen mode to use (normal or desktop). Generally the desktop is recommended, as it is less restrictive than normal mode on some operating systems.

table.name: usedpiscale
table.description: Sets whetever to enable or disable automatic DPI scaling.

table.name: vsync
table.description:
Enables or deactivates vertical synchronization. Vsync tries to keep the game at a steady framerate and can prevent issues like screen tearing. It is recommended to keep vsync activated if you don't know about the possible implications of turning it off. Before LÖVE 11.0, this value was boolean (true or false). Since LÖVE 11.0, this value is number (1 to enable vsync, 0 to disable vsync, -1 to use adaptive vsync when supported).

Note that in iOS, vertical synchronization is always enabled and cannot be changed.

table.name: depth
table.description: The number of bits per sample in the depth buffer (16/24/32, default nil)

table.name: stencil
table.description: Then number of bits per sample in the depth buffer (generally 8, default nil)

table.name: msaa
table.description: The number of samples to use with multi-sampled antialiasing.

table.name: display
table.description: The index of the display to show the window in, if multiple monitors are available.

table.name: highdpi
table.description: See love.window.getPixelScale, love.window.toPixels, and love.window.fromPixels. It is recommended to keep this option disabled if you can't test your game on a Mac or iOS system with a Retina display, because code will need tweaking to make sure things look correct.

table.name: x
table.description: Determines the position of the window on the user's screen. Alternatively love.window.setPosition can be used to change the position on the fly.

table.name: y
table.description: Determines the position of the window on the user's screen. Alternatively love.window.setPosition can be used to change the position on the fly.

table.name: modules
table.description: Module options.

table.name: audio
table.description: Enable the audio module.

table.name: event
table.description: Enable the event module.

table.name: graphics
table.description: Enable the graphics module.

table.name: image
table.description: Enable the image module.

table.name: joystick
table.description: Enable the joystick module.

table.name: keyboard
table.description: Enable the keyboard module.

table.name: math
table.description: Enable the math module.

table.name: mouse
table.description: Enable the mouse module.

table.name: physics
table.description: Enable the physics module.

table.name: sound
table.description: Enable the sound module.

table.name: system
table.description: Enable the system module.

table.name: timer
table.description: Enable the timer module.

table.name: touch
table.description: Enable the touch module.

table.name: video
table.description: Enable the video module.

table.name: window
table.description: Enable the window module.

table.name: thread
table.description: Enable the thread module.

entries.name: compress
entries.description: Compresses a string or data using a specific compression algorithm.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the compressed data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format to use when compressing the string.

arguments.name: rawstring
arguments.description: The raw (un-compressed) string to compress.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: The level of compression to use, between 0 and 9. -1 indicates the default level. The meaning of this argument depends on the compression format being used.

returns.name: compressedData
returns.description: CompressedData/string which contains the compressed version of rawstring.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the compressed data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format to use when compressing the data.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: A Data object containing the raw (un-compressed) data to compress.

arguments.name: level
arguments.description: The level of compression to use, between 0 and 9. -1 indicates the default level. The meaning of this argument depends on the compression format being used.

returns.name: compressedData
returns.description: CompressedData/string which contains the compressed version of data.

entries.name: decode
entries.description: Decode Data or a string from any of the EncodeFormats to Data or string.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the decoded data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format of the input data.

arguments.name: sourceString
arguments.description: The raw (encoded) data to decode.

returns.name: decoded
returns.description: ByteData/string which contains the decoded version of source.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the decoded data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format of the input data.

arguments.name: sourceData
arguments.description: The raw (encoded) data to decode.

returns.name: decoded
returns.description: ByteData/string which contains the decoded version of source.

entries.name: decompress
entries.description: Decompresses a CompressedData or previously compressed string or Data object.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the decompressed data as.

arguments.name: compressedData
arguments.description: The compressed data to decompress.

returns.name: decompressedData
returns.description: Data/string containing the raw decompressed data.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the decompressed data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format that was used to compress the given string.

arguments.name: compressedString
arguments.description: A string containing data previously compressed with love.data.compress.

returns.name: decompressedData
returns.description: Data/string containing the raw decompressed data.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the decompressed data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format that was used to compress the given data.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: A Data object containing data previously compressed with love.data.compress.

returns.name: decompressedData
returns.description: Data/string containing the raw decompressed data.

entries.name: encode
entries.description: Encode Data or a string to a Data or string in one of the EncodeFormats.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the encoded data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format of the output data.

arguments.name: sourceString
arguments.description: The raw data to encode.

arguments.name: linelength
arguments.description: The maximum line length of the output. Only supported for base64, ignored if 0.

returns.name: encoded
returns.description: ByteData/string which contains the encoded version of source.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the encoded data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: The format of the output data.

arguments.name: sourceData
arguments.description: The raw data to encode.

arguments.name: linelength
arguments.description: The maximum line length of the output. Only supported for base64, ignored if 0.

returns.name: encoded
returns.description: ByteData/string which contains the encoded version of source.

entries.name: getPackedSize
entries.description:
Gets the size in bytes that a given format used with love.data.pack will use.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.packsize.

variants.description: The format string cannot have the variable-length options 's' or 'z'.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: A string determining how the values are packed. Follows the rules of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

returns.name: size
returns.description: The size in bytes that the packed data will use.

entries.name: hash
entries.description: Compute the message digest of a string using a specified hash algorithm.

arguments.name: hashFunction
arguments.description: Hash algorithm to use.

arguments.name: string
arguments.description: String to hash.

returns.name: rawdigest
returns.description: Raw message digest string.

variants.description:
To return the hex string representation of the hash, use love.data.encode

hexDigestString = love.data.encode('string', 'hex', love.data.hash(algo, data))

arguments.name: hashFunction
arguments.description: Hash algorithm to use.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: Data to hash.

returns.name: rawdigest
returns.description: Raw message digest string.

entries.name: newByteData
entries.description:
Creates a new Data object containing arbitrary bytes.

Data:getPointer along with LuaJIT's FFI can be used to manipulate the contents of the ByteData object after it has been created.

variants.description: Creates a new ByteData by copying the contents of the specified string.

arguments.name: datastring
arguments.description: The byte string to copy.

returns.name: bytedata
returns.description: The new Data object.

variants.description: Creates a new ByteData by copying from an existing Data object.

arguments.name: Data
arguments.description: The existing Data object to copy.

arguments.name: offset
arguments.description: The offset of the subsection to copy, in bytes.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: The size in bytes of the new Data object.

returns.name: bytedata
returns.description: The new Data object.

variants.description: Creates a new empty ByteData with the specific size.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: The size in bytes of the new Data object.

returns.name: bytedata
returns.description: The new Data object.

entries.name: newDataView
entries.description: Creates a new Data referencing a subsection of an existing Data object.

variants.description: Data:getString and Data:getPointer will return the original Data object's contents, with the view's offset and size applied.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The Data object to reference.

arguments.name: offset
arguments.description: The offset of the subsection to reference, in bytes.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: The size in bytes of the subsection to reference.

returns.name: view
returns.description: The new Data view.

entries.name: pack
entries.description:
Packs (serializes) simple Lua values.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.pack.

variants.description: Packing integers with values greater than 2^52 is not supported, as Lua 5.1 cannot represent those values in its number type.

arguments.name: container
arguments.description: What type to return the encoded data as.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: A string determining how the values are packed. Follows the rules of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

arguments.name: v1
arguments.description: The first value (number, boolean, or string) to serialize.
Additional values to serialize.

returns.name: data
returns.description: Data/string which contains the serialized data.

entries.name: unpack
entries.description:
Unpacks (deserializes) a byte-string or Data into simple Lua values.

This function behaves the same as Lua 5.3's string.unpack.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: A string determining how the values were packed. Follows the rules of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

arguments.name: datastring
arguments.description: A string containing the packed (serialized) data.

arguments.name: pos
arguments.description: Where to start reading in the string. Negative values can be used to read relative from the end of the string.

returns.name: v1
returns.description: The first value (number, boolean, or string) that was unpacked.
Additional unpacked values.

returns.name: index
returns.description: The index of the first unread byte in the data string.

variants.description: Unpacking integers with values greater than 2^52 is not supported, as Lua 5.1 cannot represent those values in its number type.

arguments.name: format
arguments.description: A string determining how the values were packed. Follows the rules of Lua 5.3's string.pack format strings.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: A Data object containing the packed (serialized) data.

arguments.name: pos
arguments.description: 1-based index indicating where to start reading in the Data. Negative values can be used to read relative from the end of the Data object.

returns.name: v1
returns.description: The first value (number, boolean, or string) that was unpacked.
Additional unpacked values.

returns.name: index
returns.description: The 1-based index of the first unread byte in the Data.

entries.name: directorydropped
entries.description: Callback function triggered when a directory is dragged and dropped onto the window.

variants.description: Paths passed into this callback are able to be used with love.filesystem.mount, which is the only way to get read access via love.filesystem to the dropped directory. love.filesystem.mount does not generally accept other full platform-dependent directory paths that haven't been dragged and dropped onto the window.

arguments.name: path
arguments.description: The full platform-dependent path to the directory. It can be used as an argument to love.filesystem.mount, in order to gain read access to the directory with love.filesystem.

entries.name: displayrotated
entries.description: Called when the device display orientation changed, for example, user rotated their phone 180 degrees.

arguments.name: index
arguments.description: The index of the display that changed orientation.

arguments.name: orientation
arguments.description: The new orientation.

entries.name: draw
entries.description: Callback function used to draw on the screen every frame.

entries.name: errorhandler
entries.description: The error handler, used to display error messages.

arguments.name: msg
arguments.description: The error message.

returns.name: mainLoop
returns.description: Function which handles one frame, including events and rendering, when called. If this is nil then LÖVE exits immediately.

entries.name: clear
entries.description: Clears the event queue.

entries.name: poll
entries.description: Returns an iterator for messages in the event queue.

returns.name: i
returns.description: Iterator function usable in a for loop.

entries.name: pump
entries.description:
Pump events into the event queue.

This is a low-level function, and is usually not called by the user, but by love.run.

Note that this does need to be called for any OS to think you're still running,

and if you want to handle OS-generated events at all (think callbacks).

entries.name: push
entries.description:
Adds an event to the event queue.

From 0.10.0 onwards, you may pass an arbitrary amount of arguments with this function, though the default callbacks don't ever use more than six.

arguments.name: n
arguments.description: The name of the event.

arguments.name: a
arguments.description: First event argument.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: Second event argument.

arguments.name: c
arguments.description: Third event argument.

arguments.name: d
arguments.description: Fourth event argument.

arguments.name: e
arguments.description: Fifth event argument.

arguments.name: f
arguments.description: Sixth event argument.
Further event arguments may follow.

entries.name: quit
entries.description:
Adds the quit event to the queue.

The quit event is a signal for the event handler to close LÖVE. It's possible to abort the exit process with the love.quit callback.

arguments.name: exitstatus
arguments.description: The program exit status to use when closing the application.

variants.description: Restarts the game without relaunching the executable. This cleanly shuts down the main Lua state instance and creates a brand new one.

arguments.name: 'restart'
arguments.description: Tells the default love.run to exit and restart the game without relaunching the executable.

entries.name: wait
entries.description: Like love.event.poll(), but blocks until there is an event in the queue.

returns.name: n
returns.description: The name of event.

returns.name: a
returns.description: First event argument.

returns.name: b
returns.description: Second event argument.

returns.name: c
returns.description: Third event argument.

returns.name: d
returns.description: Fourth event argument.

returns.name: e
returns.description: Fifth event argument.

returns.name: f
returns.description: Sixth event argument.
Further event arguments may follow.

entries.name: filedropped
entries.description: Callback function triggered when a file is dragged and dropped onto the window.

arguments.name: file
arguments.description: The unopened File object representing the file that was dropped.

entries.name: append
entries.description: Append data to an existing file.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name (and path) of the file.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The string data to append to the file.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: How many bytes to write.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the operation was successful, or nil if there was an error.

returns.name: errormsg
returns.description: The error message on failure.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name (and path) of the file.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The Data object to append to the file.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: How many bytes to write.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the operation was successful, or nil if there was an error.

returns.name: errormsg
returns.description: The error message on failure.

entries.name: areSymlinksEnabled
entries.description: Gets whether love.filesystem follows symbolic links.

returns.name: enable
returns.description: Whether love.filesystem follows symbolic links.

entries.name: createDirectory
entries.description:
Recursively creates a directory.

When called with 'a/b' it creates both 'a' and 'a/b', if they don't exist already.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The directory to create.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the directory was created, false if not.

entries.name: getAppdataDirectory
entries.description: Returns the application data directory (could be the same as getUserDirectory)

returns.name: path
returns.description: The path of the application data directory

entries.name: getCRequirePath
entries.description:
Gets the filesystem paths that will be searched for c libraries when require is called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory separators.) Additionally, any occurrence of a double question mark ('??') will be replaced by the name passed to require and the default library extension for the platform.

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

variants.description: The default paths string is '??', which makes require('cool') try to load cool.dll, or cool.so depending on the platform.

returns.name: paths
returns.description: The paths that the ''require'' function will check for c libraries in love's filesystem.

entries.name: getDirectoryItems
entries.description:
Returns a table with the names of files and subdirectories in the specified path. The table is not sorted in any way; the order is undefined.

If the path passed to the function exists in the game and the save directory, it will list the files and directories from both places.

arguments.name: dir
arguments.description: The directory.

returns.name: files
returns.description: A sequence with the names of all files and subdirectories as strings.

arguments.name: dir
arguments.description: The directory.

arguments.name: callback
arguments.description: A function which is called for each file and folder in the directory. The filename is passed to the function as an argument.

returns.name: files
returns.description: A sequence with the names of all files and subdirectories as strings.

entries.name: getIdentity
entries.description:
Gets the write directory name for your game. 

Note that this only returns the name of the folder to store your files in, not the full path.

returns.name: name
returns.description: The identity that is used as write directory.

entries.name: getInfo
entries.description: Gets information about the specified file or directory.

arguments.name: path
arguments.description: The file or directory path to check.

arguments.name: filtertype
arguments.description: If supplied, this parameter causes getInfo to only return the info table if the item at the given path matches the specified file type.

returns.name: info
returns.description: A table containing information about the specified path, or nil if nothing exists at the path. The table contains the following fields:

table.name: type
table.description: The type of the object at the path (file, directory, symlink, etc.)

table.name: size
table.description: The size in bytes of the file, or nil if it can't be determined.

table.name: modtime
table.description: The file's last modification time in seconds since the unix epoch, or nil if it can't be determined.

variants.description: This variant accepts an existing table to fill in, instead of creating a new one.

arguments.name: path
arguments.description: The file or directory path to check.

arguments.name: info
arguments.description: A table which will be filled in with info about the specified path.

returns.name: info
returns.description: The table given as an argument, or nil if nothing exists at the path. The table will be filled in with the following fields:

table.name: type
table.description: The type of the object at the path (file, directory, symlink, etc.)

table.name: size
table.description: The size in bytes of the file, or nil if it can't be determined.

table.name: modtime
table.description: The file's last modification time in seconds since the unix epoch, or nil if it can't be determined.

variants.description: This variant only returns info if the item at the given path is the same file type as specified in the filtertype argument, and accepts an existing table to fill in, instead of creating a new one.

arguments.name: path
arguments.description: The file or directory path to check.

arguments.name: filtertype
arguments.description: Causes getInfo to only return the info table if the item at the given path matches the specified file type.

arguments.name: info
arguments.description: A table which will be filled in with info about the specified path.

returns.name: info
returns.description: The table given as an argument, or nil if nothing exists at the path. The table will be filled in with the following fields:

table.name: type
table.description: The type of the object at the path (file, directory, symlink, etc.)

table.name: size
table.description: The size in bytes of the file, or nil if it can't be determined.

table.name: modtime
table.description: The file's last modification time in seconds since the unix epoch, or nil if it can't be determined.

entries.name: getRealDirectory
entries.description:
Gets the platform-specific absolute path of the directory containing a filepath.

This can be used to determine whether a file is inside the save directory or the game's source .love.

variants.description: This function returns the directory containing the given ''file path'', rather than file. For example, if the file screenshot1.png exists in a directory called screenshots in the game's save directory, love.filesystem.getRealDirectory('screenshots/screenshot1.png') will return the same value as love.filesystem.getSaveDirectory.

arguments.name: filepath
arguments.description: The filepath to get the directory of.

returns.name: realdir
returns.description: The platform-specific full path of the directory containing the filepath.

entries.name: getRequirePath
entries.description:
Gets the filesystem paths that will be searched when require is called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory separators.)

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

variants.description: The default paths string is '?.lua;?/init.lua', which makes require('cool') try to load cool.lua and then try cool/init.lua if cool.lua doesn't exist.

returns.name: paths
returns.description: The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

entries.name: getSaveDirectory
entries.description:
Gets the full path to the designated save directory.

This can be useful if you want to use the standard io library (or something else) to

read or write in the save directory.

returns.name: dir
returns.description: The absolute path to the save directory.

entries.name: getSource
entries.description: Returns the full path to the the .love file or directory. If the game is fused to the LÖVE executable, then the executable is returned.

returns.name: path
returns.description: The full platform-dependent path of the .love file or directory.

entries.name: getSourceBaseDirectory
entries.description:
Returns the full path to the directory containing the .love file. If the game is fused to the LÖVE executable, then the directory containing the executable is returned.

If love.filesystem.isFused is true, the path returned by this function can be passed to love.filesystem.mount, which will make the directory containing the main game (e.g. C:\Program Files\coolgame\) readable by love.filesystem.

returns.name: path
returns.description: The full platform-dependent path of the directory containing the .love file.

entries.name: getUserDirectory
entries.description: Returns the path of the user's directory

returns.name: path
returns.description: The path of the user's directory

entries.name: getWorkingDirectory
entries.description: Gets the current working directory.

returns.name: cwd
returns.description: The current working directory.

entries.name: init
entries.description: Initializes love.filesystem, will be called internally, so should not be used explicitly.

arguments.name: appname
arguments.description: The name of the application binary, typically love.

entries.name: isFused
entries.description:
Gets whether the game is in fused mode or not.

If a game is in fused mode, its save directory will be directly in the Appdata directory instead of Appdata/LOVE/. The game will also be able to load C Lua dynamic libraries which are located in the save directory.

A game is in fused mode if the source .love has been fused to the executable (see Game Distribution), or if '--fused' has been given as a command-line argument when starting the game.

returns.name: fused
returns.description: True if the game is in fused mode, false otherwise.

entries.name: lines
entries.description: Iterate over the lines in a file.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name (and path) of the file

returns.name: iterator
returns.description: A function that iterates over all the lines in the file

entries.name: load
entries.description: Loads a Lua file (but does not run it).

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The name (and path) of the file.

returns.name: chunk
returns.description: The loaded chunk.

returns.name: errormsg
returns.description: The error message if file could not be opened.

entries.name: mount
entries.description:
Mounts a zip file or folder in the game's save directory for reading.

It is also possible to mount love.filesystem.getSourceBaseDirectory if the game is in fused mode.

arguments.name: archive
arguments.description: The folder or zip file in the game's save directory to mount.

arguments.name: mountpoint
arguments.description: The new path the archive will be mounted to.

arguments.name: appendToPath
arguments.description: Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source and save directories.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

variants.description: Mounts the contents of the given FileData in memory. The FileData's data must contain a zipped directory structure.

arguments.name: filedata
arguments.description: The FileData object in memory to mount.

arguments.name: mountpoint
arguments.description: The new path the archive will be mounted to.

arguments.name: appendToPath
arguments.description: Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source and save directories.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

variants.description: Mounts the contents of the given Data object in memory. The data must contain a zipped directory structure.

arguments.name: data
arguments.description: The Data object in memory to mount.

arguments.name: archivename
arguments.description: The name to associate the mounted data with, for use with love.filesystem.unmount. Must be unique compared to other mounted data.

arguments.name: mountpoint
arguments.description: The new path the archive will be mounted to.

arguments.name: appendToPath
arguments.description: Whether the archive will be searched when reading a filepath before or after already-mounted archives. This includes the game's source and save directories.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the archive was successfully mounted, false otherwise.

entries.name: newFile
entries.description:
Creates a new File object. 

It needs to be opened before it can be accessed.

variants.description: Please note that this function will not return any error message (e.g. if you use an invalid filename) because it just creates the File Object. You can still check if the file is valid by using File:open which returns a boolean and an error message if something goes wrong while opening the file.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filename of the file.

returns.name: file
returns.description: The new File object.

variants.description: Creates a File object and opens it for reading, writing, or appending.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filename of the file.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: The mode to open the file in.

returns.name: file
returns.description: The new File object, or nil if an error occurred.

returns.name: errorstr
returns.description: The error string if an error occurred.

entries.name: newFileData
entries.description: Creates a new FileData object from a file on disk, or from a string in memory.

variants.description: Creates a new FileData object from a string in memory.

arguments.name: contents
arguments.description: The contents of the file in memory represented as a string.

entries.name: remove
entries.description: Removes a file or empty directory.

variants.description:
The directory must be empty before removal or else it will fail. Simply remove all files and folders in the directory beforehand.

If the file exists in the .love but not in the save directory, it returns false as well.

An opened File prevents removal of the underlying file. Simply close the File to remove it.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The file or directory to remove.

returns.name: success
returns.description: True if the file/directory was removed, false otherwise.

entries.name: setCRequirePath
entries.description:
Sets the filesystem paths that will be searched for c libraries when require is called.

The paths string returned by this function is a sequence of path templates separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory separators.) Additionally, any occurrence of a double question mark ('??') will be replaced by the name passed to require and the default library extension for the platform.

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

variants.description: The default paths string is '??', which makes require('cool') try to load cool.dll, or cool.so depending on the platform.

arguments.name: paths
arguments.description: The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

entries.name: setIdentity
entries.description:
Sets the write directory for your game. 

Note that you can only set the name of the folder to store your files in, not the location.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The new identity that will be used as write directory.

arguments.name: name
arguments.description: The new identity that will be used as write directory.

entries.name: setRequirePath
entries.description:
Sets the filesystem paths that will be searched when require is called.

The paths string given to this function is a sequence of path templates separated by semicolons. The argument passed to ''require'' will be inserted in place of any question mark ('?') character in each template (after the dot characters in the argument passed to ''require'' are replaced by directory separators.)

The paths are relative to the game's source and save directories, as well as any paths mounted with love.filesystem.mount.

variants.description: The default paths string is '?.lua;?/init.lua', which makes require('cool') try to load cool.lua and then try cool/init.lua if cool.lua doesn't exist.

arguments.name: paths
arguments.description: The paths that the ''require'' function will check in love's filesystem.

entries.name: captureScreenshot
entries.description:
Creates a screenshot once the current frame is done (after love.draw has finished).

Since this function enqueues a screenshot capture rather than executing it immediately, it can be called from an input callback or love.update and it will still capture all of what's drawn to the screen in that frame.

arguments.description: The filename to save the screenshot to. The encoded image type is determined based on the extension of the filename, and must be one of the ImageFormats.

arguments.description: Function which gets called once the screenshot has been captured. An ImageData is passed into the function as its only argument.

entries.name: clear
entries.description:
Clears the screen or active Canvas to the specified color.

This function is called automatically before love.draw in the default love.run function. See the example in love.run for a typical use of this function.

Note that the scissor area bounds the cleared region.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

In versions prior to background color instead.

variants.description: Clears the screen to the background color in 0.9.2 and earlier, or to transparent black (0, 0, 0, 0) in LÖVE 0.10.0 and newer.
Clears the screen or active Canvas to the specified color.

A color must be specified for each active Canvas, when this function variant is used.

entries.name: discard
entries.description:
Discards (trashes) the contents of the screen or active Canvas. This is a performance optimization function with niche use cases.

If the active Canvas has just been changed and the 'replace' BlendMode is about to be used to draw something which covers the entire screen, calling love.graphics.discard rather than calling love.graphics.clear or doing nothing may improve performance on mobile devices.

On some desktop systems this function may do nothing.

arguments.name: discardcolor
arguments.description: Whether to discard the texture(s) of the active Canvas(es) (the contents of the screen if no Canvas is active.)

arguments.name: discardstencil
arguments.description: Whether to discard the contents of the stencil buffer of the screen / active Canvas.

arguments.name: discardcolors
arguments.description: An array containing boolean values indicating whether to discard the texture of each active Canvas, when multiple simultaneous Canvases are active.

arguments.name: discardstencil
arguments.description: Whether to discard the contents of the stencil buffer of the screen / active Canvas.

entries.name: draw
entries.description:
Draws a Drawable object (an Image, Canvas, SpriteBatch, ParticleSystem, Mesh, Text object, or Video) on the screen with optional rotation, scaling and shearing.

Objects are drawn relative to their local coordinate system. The origin is by default located at the top left corner of Image and Canvas. All scaling, shearing, and rotation arguments transform the object relative to that point. Also, the position of the origin can be specified on the screen coordinate system.

It's possible to rotate an object about its center by offsetting the origin to the center. Angles must be given in radians for rotation. One can also use a negative scaling factor to flip about its centerline. 

Note that the offsets are applied before rotation, scaling, or shearing; scaling and shearing are applied before rotation.

The right and bottom edges of the object are shifted at an angle defined by the shearing factors.

When using the default shader anything drawn with this function will be tinted according to the currently selected color.  Set it to pure white to preserve the object's original colors.

entries.name: drawInstanced
entries.description:
Draws many instances of a Mesh with a single draw call, using hardware geometry instancing.

Each instance can have unique properties (positions, colors, etc.) but will not by default unless a custom per-instance vertex attributes or the love_InstanceID GLSL 3 vertex shader variable is used, otherwise they will all render at the same position on top of each other.

Instancing is not supported by some older GPUs that are only capable of using OpenGL ES 2 or OpenGL 2. Use love.graphics.getSupported to check.
