entries.name: flushBatch
entries.description:
Immediately renders any pending automatically batched draws.

LÖVE will call this function internally as needed when most state is changed, so it is not necessary to manually call it.

The current batch will be automatically flushed by love.graphics state changes (except for the transform stack and the current color), as well as Shader:send and methods on Textures which change their state. Using a different Image in consecutive love.graphics.draw calls will also flush the current batch.

SpriteBatches, ParticleSystems, Meshes, and Text objects do their own batching and do not affect automatic batching of other draws, aside from flushing the current batch when they're drawn.

entries.name: getBackgroundColor
entries.description:
Gets the current background color.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

returns.name: r
returns.description: The red component (0-1).

returns.name: g
returns.description: The green component (0-1).

returns.name: b
returns.description: The blue component (0-1).

returns.name: a
returns.description: The alpha component (0-1).

entries.name: getBlendMode
entries.description: Gets the blending mode.

returns.name: mode
returns.description: The current blend mode.

returns.name: alphamode
returns.description: The current blend alpha mode – it determines how the alpha of drawn objects affects blending.

entries.name: getCanvas
entries.description: Gets the current target Canvas.

returns.name: canvas
returns.description: The Canvas set by setCanvas. Returns nil if drawing to the real screen.

entries.name: getCanvasFormats
entries.description: Gets the available Canvas formats, and whether each is supported.

returns.name: formats
returns.description: A table containing CanvasFormats as keys, and a boolean indicating whether the format is supported as values. Not all systems support all formats.

arguments.name: readable
arguments.description: If true, the returned formats will only be indicated as supported if readable flag set to true for that format, and vice versa if the parameter is false.

returns.name: formats
returns.description: A table containing CanvasFormats as keys, and a boolean indicating whether the format is supported as values (taking into account the readable parameter). Not all systems support all formats.

entries.name: getColor
entries.description:
Gets the current color.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

returns.name: r
returns.description: The red component (0-1).

returns.name: g
returns.description: The green component (0-1).

returns.name: b
returns.description: The blue component (0-1).

returns.name: a
returns.description: The alpha component (0-1).

entries.name: getColorMask
entries.description:
Gets the active color components used when drawing. Normally all 4 components are active unless love.graphics.setColorMask has been used.

The color mask determines whether individual components of the colors of drawn objects will affect the color of the screen. They affect love.graphics.clear and Canvas:clear as well.

returns.name: r
returns.description: Whether the red color component is active when rendering.

returns.name: g
returns.description: Whether the green color component is active when rendering.

returns.name: b
returns.description: Whether the blue color component is active when rendering.

returns.name: a
returns.description: Whether the alpha color component is active when rendering.

entries.name: getDefaultFilter
entries.description: Returns the default scaling filters used with Images, Canvases, and Fonts.

returns.name: min
returns.description: Filter mode used when scaling the image down.

returns.name: mag
returns.description: Filter mode used when scaling the image up.

returns.name: anisotropy
returns.description: Maximum amount of Anisotropic Filtering used.

entries.name: getDepthMode
entries.description:
Gets the current depth test mode and whether writing to the depth buffer is enabled.

This is low-level functionality designed for use with custom vertex shaders and Meshes with custom vertex attributes. No higher level APIs are provided to set the depth of 2D graphics such as shapes, lines, and Images.

returns.name: comparemode
returns.description: Depth comparison mode used for depth testing.

returns.name: write
returns.description: Whether to write update / write values to the depth buffer when rendering.

entries.name: getDimensions
entries.description: Gets the width and height in pixels of the window.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the window.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the window.

entries.name: getDPIScale
entries.description:
Gets the DPI scale factor of the window.

The DPI scale factor represents relative pixel density. The pixel density inside the window might be greater (or smaller) than the 'size' of the window. For example on a retina screen in Mac OS X with the highdpi window flag enabled, the window may take up the same physical size as an 800x600 window, but the area inside the window uses 1600x1200 pixels. love.graphics.getDPIScale() would return 2 in that case.

The love.window.fromPixels and love.window.toPixels functions can also be used to convert between units.

The highdpi window flag must be enabled to use the full pixel density of a Retina screen on Mac OS X and iOS. The flag currently does nothing on Windows and Linux, and on Android it is effectively always enabled.

variants.description: The units of love.graphics.getWidth, love.graphics.getHeight, love.mouse.getPosition, mouse events, love.touch.getPosition, and touch events are always in DPI-scaled units rather than pixels. In LÖVE 0.10 and older they were in pixels.

returns.name: scale
returns.description: The pixel scale factor associated with the window.

entries.name: getFont
entries.description: Gets the current Font object.

returns.name: font
returns.description: The current Font. Automatically creates and sets the default font, if none is set yet.

entries.name: getFrontFaceWinding
entries.description:
Gets whether triangles with clockwise- or counterclockwise-ordered vertices are considered front-facing.

This is designed for use in combination with Mesh face culling. Other love.graphics shapes, lines, and sprites are not guaranteed to have a specific winding order to their internal vertices.

returns.name: winding
returns.description: The winding mode being used. The default winding is counterclockwise ('ccw').

entries.name: getHeight
entries.description: Gets the height in pixels of the window.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the window.

entries.name: getImageFormats
entries.description: Gets the raw and compressed pixel formats usable for Images, and whether each is supported.

returns.name: formats
returns.description: A table containing PixelFormats as keys, and a boolean indicating whether the format is supported as values. Not all systems support all formats.

entries.name: getLineJoin
entries.description: Gets the line join style.

returns.name: join
returns.description: The LineJoin style.

entries.name: getLineStyle
entries.description: Gets the line style.

returns.name: style
returns.description: The current line style.

entries.name: getLineWidth
entries.description: Gets the current line width.

variants.description:
This function does not work in 0.8.0, but has been fixed in version 0.9.0. Use the following snippet to circumvent this in 0.8.0;

love.graphics._getLineWidth = love.graphics.getLineWidth

love.graphics._setLineWidth = love.graphics.setLineWidth

function love.graphics.getLineWidth() return love.graphics.varlinewidth or 1 end

function love.graphics.setLineWidth(w) love.graphics.varlinewidth = w; return love.graphics._setLineWidth(w) end

returns.name: width
returns.description: The current line width.

entries.name: getMeshCullMode
entries.description:
Gets whether back-facing triangles in a Mesh are culled.

Mesh face culling is designed for use with low level custom hardware-accelerated 3D rendering via custom vertex attributes on Meshes, custom vertex shaders, and depth testing with a depth buffer.

returns.name: mode
returns.description: The Mesh face culling mode in use (whether to render everything, cull back-facing triangles, or cull front-facing triangles).

entries.name: getPixelDimensions
entries.description:
Gets the width and height in pixels of the window.

love.graphics.getDimensions gets the dimensions of the window in units scaled by the screen's DPI scale factor, rather than pixels. Use getDimensions for calculations related to drawing to the screen and using the graphics coordinate system (calculating the center of the screen, for example), and getPixelDimensions only when dealing specifically with underlying pixels (pixel-related calculations in a pixel Shader, for example).

returns.name: pixelwidth
returns.description: The width of the window in pixels.

returns.name: pixelheight
returns.description: The height of the window in pixels.

entries.name: getPixelHeight
entries.description:
Gets the height in pixels of the window.

The graphics coordinate system and DPI scale factor, rather than raw pixels. Use getHeight for calculations related to drawing to the screen and using the coordinate system (calculating the center of the screen, for example), and getPixelHeight only when dealing specifically with underlying pixels (pixel-related calculations in a pixel Shader, for example).

returns.name: pixelheight
returns.description: The height of the window in pixels.

entries.name: getPixelWidth
entries.description:
Gets the width in pixels of the window.

The graphics coordinate system and DPI scale factor, rather than raw pixels. Use getWidth for calculations related to drawing to the screen and using the coordinate system (calculating the center of the screen, for example), and getPixelWidth only when dealing specifically with underlying pixels (pixel-related calculations in a pixel Shader, for example).

returns.name: pixelwidth
returns.description: The width of the window in pixels.

entries.name: getPointSize
entries.description: Gets the point size.

returns.name: size
returns.description: The current point size.

entries.name: getRendererInfo
entries.description: Gets information about the system's video card and drivers.

returns.name: name
returns.description: The name of the renderer, e.g. 'OpenGL' or 'OpenGL ES'.

returns.name: version
returns.description: The version of the renderer with some extra driver-dependent version info, e.g. '2.1 INTEL-8.10.44'.

returns.name: vendor
returns.description: The name of the graphics card vendor, e.g. 'Intel Inc'.

returns.name: device
returns.description: The name of the graphics card, e.g. 'Intel HD Graphics 3000 OpenGL Engine'.

entries.name: getScissor
entries.description: Gets the current scissor box.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The x-component of the top-left point of the box.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The y-component of the top-left point of the box.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the box.

returns.name: height
returns.description: The height of the box.

entries.name: getShader
entries.description: Gets the current Shader. Returns nil if none is set.

returns.name: shader
returns.description: The currently active Shader, or nil if none is set.

entries.name: getStackDepth
entries.description: Gets the current depth of the transform / state stack (the number of pushes without corresponding pops).

returns.name: depth
returns.description: The current depth of the transform and state love.graphics stack.

entries.name: getStats
entries.description: Gets performance-related rendering statistics.

returns.name: stats
returns.description: A table with the following fields:

table.name: drawcalls
table.description: The number of draw calls made so far during the current frame.

table.name: canvasswitches
table.description: The number of times the active Canvas has been switched so far during the current frame.

table.name: texturememory
table.description: The estimated total size in bytes of video memory used by all loaded Images, Canvases, and Fonts.

table.name: images
table.description: The number of Image objects currently loaded.

table.name: canvases
table.description: The number of Canvas objects currently loaded.

table.name: fonts
table.description: The number of Font objects currently loaded.

table.name: shaderswitches
table.description: The number of times the active Shader has been changed so far during the current frame.

table.name: drawcallsbatched
table.description: The number of draw calls that were saved by LÖVE's automatic batching, since the start of the frame.

variants.description: This variant accepts an existing table to fill in, instead of creating a new one.

arguments.name: stats
arguments.description: A table which will be filled in with the stat fields below.

returns.name: stats
returns.description: The table that was passed in above, now containing the following fields:

table.name: drawcalls
table.description: The number of draw calls made so far during the current frame.

table.name: canvasswitches
table.description: The number of times the active Canvas has been switched so far during the current frame.

table.name: texturememory
table.description: The estimated total size in bytes of video memory used by all loaded Images, Canvases, and Fonts.

table.name: images
table.description: The number of Image objects currently loaded.

table.name: canvases
table.description: The number of Canvas objects currently loaded.

table.name: fonts
table.description: The number of Font objects currently loaded.

table.name: shaderswitches
table.description: The number of times the active Shader has been changed so far during the current frame.

table.name: drawcallsbatched
table.description: The number of draw calls that were saved by LÖVE's automatic batching, since the start of the frame.

entries.name: getStencilTest
entries.description:
Gets the current stencil test configuration.

When stencil testing is enabled, the geometry of everything that is drawn afterward will be clipped / stencilled out based on a comparison between the arguments of this function and the stencil value of each pixel that the geometry touches. The stencil values of pixels are affected via love.graphics.stencil.

Each Canvas has its own per-pixel stencil values.

returns.name: comparemode
returns.description: The type of comparison that is made for each pixel. Will be 'always' if stencil testing is disabled.

returns.name: comparevalue
returns.description: The value used when comparing with the stencil value of each pixel.

entries.name: getSupported
entries.description:
Gets the optional graphics features and whether they're supported on the system.

Some older or low-end systems don't always support all graphics features.

returns.name: features
returns.description: A table containing GraphicsFeature keys, and boolean values indicating whether each feature is supported.

entries.name: getSystemLimits
entries.description: Gets the system-dependent maximum values for love.graphics features.

returns.name: limits
returns.description: A table containing GraphicsLimit keys, and number values.

entries.name: getTextureTypes
entries.description: Gets the available texture types, and whether each is supported.

returns.name: texturetypes
returns.description: A table containing TextureTypes as keys, and a boolean indicating whether the type is supported as values. Not all systems support all types.

entries.name: getWidth
entries.description: Gets the width in pixels of the window.

returns.name: width
returns.description: The width of the window.

entries.name: intersectScissor
entries.description:
Sets the scissor to the rectangle created by the intersection of the specified rectangle with the existing scissor.  If no scissor is active yet, it behaves like love.graphics.setScissor.

The scissor limits the drawing area to a specified rectangle. This affects all graphics calls, including love.graphics.clear.

The dimensions of the scissor is unaffected by graphical transformations (translate, scale, ...).

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x-coordinate of the upper left corner of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y-coordinate of the upper left corner of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height of the rectangle to intersect with the existing scissor rectangle.

entries.name: inverseTransformPoint
entries.description:
Converts the given 2D position from screen-space into global coordinates.

This effectively applies the reverse of the current graphics transformations to the given position. A similar Transform:inverseTransformPoint method exists for Transform objects.

arguments.name: screenX
arguments.description: The x component of the screen-space position.

arguments.name: screenY
arguments.description: The y component of the screen-space position.

returns.name: globalX
returns.description: The x component of the position in global coordinates.

returns.name: globalY
returns.description: The y component of the position in global coordinates.

entries.name: isActive
entries.description:
Gets whether the graphics module is able to be used. If it is not active, love.graphics function and method calls will not work correctly and may cause the program to crash.
The graphics module is inactive if a window is not open, or if the app is in the background on iOS. Typically the app's execution will be automatically paused by the system, in the latter case.

returns.name: active
returns.description: Whether the graphics module is active and able to be used.

entries.name: isGammaCorrect
entries.description:
Gets whether gamma-correct rendering is supported and enabled. It can be enabled by setting t.gammacorrect = true in love.conf.

Not all devices support gamma-correct rendering, in which case it will be automatically disabled and this function will return false. It is supported on desktop systems which have graphics cards that are capable of using OpenGL 3 / DirectX 10, and iOS devices that can use OpenGL ES 3.

variants.description:
When gamma-correct rendering is enabled, many functions and objects perform automatic color conversion between sRGB and linear RGB in order for blending and shader math to be mathematically correct (which they aren't if it's not enabled.)

* The colors passed into converted from sRGB to linear RGB.

* The colors set in text with per-character colors, points with per-point colors, standard custom Meshes which use the 'VertexColor' attribute name will automatically be converted from sRGB to linear RGB when those objects are drawn.

* creating the Image.

* Everything drawn to the screen will be blended in linear RGB and then the result will be converted to sRGB for display.

* Canvases which use the 'normal' or 'srgb' CanvasFormat will have their content blended in linear RGB and the result will be stored in the canvas in sRGB, when drawing to them. When the Canvas itself is drawn, its pixel colors will be converted from sRGB to linear RGB in the same manner as Images. Keeping the canvas pixel data stored as sRGB allows for better precision (less banding) for darker colors compared to 'rgba8'.

Because most conversions are automatically handled, your own code doesn't need to worry about sRGB and linear RGB color conversions when gamma-correct rendering is enabled, except in a couple cases:

* If a Mesh with custom vertex attributes is used and one of the attributes is meant to be used as a color in a Shader, and the attribute isn't named 'VertexColor'.

* If  a Shader is used which has uniform / extern variables or other variables that are meant to be used as colors, and Shader:sendColor isn't used.

In both cases, love.math.gammaToLinear can be used to convert color values to linear RGB in Lua code, or the gammaCorrectColor (or unGammaCorrectColor if necessary) shader functions can be used inside shader code. Those shader functions ''only'' do conversions if gamma-correct rendering is actually enabled. The LOVE_GAMMA_CORRECT shader preprocessor define will be set if so.

Read more about gamma-correct rendering here, here, and here.

returns.name: gammacorrect
returns.description: True if gamma-correct rendering is supported and was enabled in love.conf, false otherwise.

entries.name: isWireframe
entries.description: Gets whether wireframe mode is used when drawing.

returns.name: wireframe
returns.description: True if wireframe lines are used when drawing, false if it's not.

entries.name: line
entries.description: Draws lines between points.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The position of first point on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The position of first point on the y-axis.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The position of second point on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The position of second point on the y-axis.
You can continue passing point positions to draw a polyline.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A table of point positions, as described above.

entries.name: newArrayImage
entries.description:
Creates a new array Image.

An array image / array texture is a single object which contains multiple 'layers' or 'slices' of 2D sub-images. It can be thought of similarly to a texture atlas or sprite sheet, but it doesn't suffer from the same tile / quad bleeding artifacts that texture atlases do – although every sub-image must have the same dimensions.

A specific layer of an array image can be drawn with love.graphics.drawLayer / SpriteBatch:addLayer, or with the Quad variant of love.graphics.draw and Quad:setLayer, or via a custom Shader.

To use an array image in a Shader, it must be declared as a ArrayImage or sampler2DArray type (instead of Image or sampler2D). The Texel(ArrayImage image, vec3 texturecoord) shader function must be used to get pixel colors from a slice of the array image. The vec3 argument contains the texture coordinate in the first two components, and the 0-based slice index in the third component.

variants.description:
Creates an array Image given a different image file for each slice of the resulting array image object.

Illustration of how an array image works: [http://codeflow.org/entries/2010/dec/09/minecraft-like-rendering-experiments-in-opengl-4/illustrations/textures.jpg]

A DPI scale of 2 (double the normal pixel density) will result in the image taking up the same space on-screen as an image with half its pixel dimensions that has a DPI scale of 1. This allows for easily swapping out image assets that take the same space on-screen but have different pixel densities, which makes supporting high-dpi / retina resolution require less code logic.

In order to use an Array Texture or other non-2D texture types as the main texture in a custom void effect() variant must be used in the pixel shader, and MainTex must be declared as an ArrayImage or sampler2DArray like so: uniform ArrayImage MainTex;.

arguments.name: slices
arguments.description: A table containing filepaths to images (or File, FileData, ImageData, or CompressedImageData objects), in an array. Each sub-image must have the same dimensions. A table of tables can also be given, where each sub-table contains all mipmap levels for the slice index of that sub-table.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the array image, containing the following fields:

table.name: mipmaps
table.description: True to make the image use mipmaps, false to disable them. Mipmaps will be automatically generated if the image isn't a compressed texture format.

table.name: linear
table.description: True to treat the image's pixels as linear instead of sRGB, when gamma correct rendering is enabled. Most images are authored as sRGB.

table.name: dpiscale
table.description: The DPI scale to use when drawing the array image and calling getWidth/getHeight.

returns.name: image
returns.description: An Array Image object.

entries.name: newCanvas
entries.description: Creates a new Canvas object for offscreen rendering.

returns.name: canvas
returns.description: A new Canvas with dimensions equal to the window's size in pixels.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The desired width of the Canvas.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The desired height of the Canvas.

returns.name: canvas
returns.description: A new Canvas with specified width and height.

variants.description:
Creates a 2D or cubemap Canvas using the given settings.

Some Canvas formats have higher system requirements than the default format. Use love.graphics.getCanvasFormats to check for support.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The desired width of the Canvas.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The desired height of the Canvas.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: A table containing the given fields:

table.name: type
table.description: The type of Canvas to create.

table.name: format
table.description: The format of the Canvas.

table.name: readable
table.description: Whether the Canvas is readable (drawable and accessible in a Shader). True by default for regular formats, false by default for depth/stencil formats.

table.name: msaa
table.description: The desired number of multisample antialiasing (MSAA) samples used when drawing to the Canvas.

table.name: dpiscale
table.description: The DPI scale factor of the Canvas, used when drawing to the Canvas as well as when drawing the Canvas to the screen.

table.name: mipmaps
table.description: Whether the Canvas has mipmaps, and whether to automatically regenerate them if so.

returns.name: canvas
returns.description: A new Canvas with specified width and height.

variants.description: Creates a volume or array texture-type Canvas.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The desired width of the Canvas.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The desired height of the Canvas.

arguments.name: layers
arguments.description: The number of array layers (if the Canvas is an Array Texture), or the volume depth (if the Canvas is a Volume Texture).

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: A table containing the given fields:

table.name: type
table.description: The type of Canvas to create.

table.name: format
table.description: The format of the Canvas.

table.name: readable
table.description: Whether the Canvas is readable (drawable and accessible in a Shader). True by default for regular formats, false by default for depth/stencil formats.

table.name: msaa
table.description: The desired number of multisample antialiasing (MSAA) samples used when drawing to the Canvas.

table.name: dpiscale
table.description: The DPI scale factor of the Canvas, used when drawing to the Canvas as well as when drawing the Canvas to the screen.

table.name: mipmaps
table.description: Whether the Canvas has mipmaps, and whether to automatically regenerate them if so.

returns.name: canvas
returns.description: A new Canvas with specified width and height.

entries.name: newCubeImage
entries.description:
Creates a new cubemap Image.

Cubemap images have 6 faces (sides) which represent a cube. They can't be rendered directly, they can only be used in Shader code (and sent to the shader via Shader:send).

To use a cubemap image in a Shader, it must be declared as a CubeImage or samplerCube type (instead of Image or sampler2D). The Texel(CubeImage image, vec3 direction) shader function must be used to get pixel colors from the cubemap. The vec3 argument is a normalized direction from the center of the cube, rather than explicit texture coordinates.

Each face in a cubemap image must have square dimensions.

For variants of this function which accept a single image containing multiple cubemap faces, they must be laid out in one of the following forms in the image:

   +y

+z +x -z

   -y

   -x

or:

   +y

-x +z +x -z

   -y

or:

+x

-x

+y

-y

+z

-z

or:

+x -x +y -y +z -z

variants.description: Creates a cubemap Image given a single image file containing multiple cube faces.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filepath to a cubemap image file (or a File, FileData, or ImageData).

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the cubemap image, containing the following fields:

table.name: mipmaps
table.description: True to make the image use mipmaps, false to disable them. Mipmaps will be automatically generated if the image isn't a compressed texture format.

table.name: linear
table.description: True to treat the image's pixels as linear instead of sRGB, when gamma correct rendering is enabled. Most images are authored as sRGB.

returns.name: image
returns.description: An cubemap Image object.

variants.description: Creates a cubemap Image given a different image file for each cube face.

arguments.name: faces
arguments.description: A table containing 6 filepaths to images (or File, FileData, ImageData, or CompressedImageData objects), in an array. Each face image must have the same dimensions. A table of tables can also be given, where each sub-table contains all mipmap levels for the cube face index of that sub-table.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the cubemap image, containing the following fields:

table.name: mipmaps
table.description: True to make the image use mipmaps, false to disable them. Mipmaps will be automatically generated if the image isn't a compressed texture format.

table.name: linear
table.description: True to treat the image's pixels as linear instead of sRGB, when gamma correct rendering is enabled. Most images are authored as sRGB.

returns.name: image
returns.description: An cubemap Image object.

entries.name: newFont
entries.description:
Creates a new Font from a TrueType Font or BMFont file. Created fonts are not cached, in that calling this function with the same arguments will always create a new Font object.

All variants which accept a filename can also accept a Data object instead.

variants.description:
Create a new BMFont or TrueType font.

If the file is a TrueType font, it will be size 12. Use the variant below to create a TrueType font with a custom size.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filepath to the BMFont or TrueType font file.

returns.name: font
returns.description: A Font object which can be used to draw text on screen.

variants.description: Create a new TrueType font.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filepath to the TrueType font file.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: The size of the font in pixels.

arguments.name: hinting
arguments.description: True Type hinting mode.

arguments.name: dpiscale
arguments.description: The DPI scale factor of the font.

returns.name: font
returns.description: A Font object which can be used to draw text on screen.

variants.description: Create a new BMFont.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filepath to the BMFont file.

arguments.name: imagefilename
arguments.description: The filepath to the BMFont's image file. If this argument is omitted, the path specified inside the BMFont file will be used.

returns.name: font
returns.description: A Font object which can be used to draw text on screen.

variants.description: Create a new instance of the default font (Vera Sans) with a custom size.

arguments.name: size
arguments.description: The size of the font in pixels.

arguments.name: hinting
arguments.description: True Type hinting mode.

arguments.name: dpiscale
arguments.description: The DPI scale factor of the font.

returns.name: font
returns.description: A Font object which can be used to draw text on screen.

entries.name: newImage
entries.description: Creates a new Image from a filepath, FileData, an ImageData, or a CompressedImageData, and optionally generates or specifies mipmaps for the image.

arguments.name: filename

entries.name: newMesh
entries.description:
Creates a new Mesh.

Use Mesh:setTexture if the Mesh should be textured with an Image or Canvas when it's drawn.

In versions prior to 11.0, color and byte component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

variants.description: Creates a standard Mesh with the specified vertices.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: The table filled with vertex information tables for each vertex as follows:

table.description: The position of the vertex on the x-axis.
The position of the vertex on the y-axis.
The u texture coordinate of the vertex. Texture coordinates are normally in the range of 1, but can be greater or less (see WrapMode.)
The v texture coordinate of the vertex. Texture coordinates are normally in the range of 1, but can be greater or less (see WrapMode.)
The red component of the vertex color.
The green component of the vertex color.
The blue component of the vertex color.
The alpha component of the vertex color.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How the vertices are used when drawing. The default mode 'fan' is sufficient for simple convex polygons.

arguments.name: usage
arguments.description: The expected usage of the Mesh. The specified usage mode affects the Mesh's memory usage and performance.

returns.name: mesh
returns.description: The new mesh.

variants.description:
Creates a standard Mesh with the specified number of vertices.

Mesh:setVertices or Mesh:setVertex and Mesh:setDrawRange can be used to specify vertex information once the Mesh is created.

arguments.name: vertexcount
arguments.description: The total number of vertices the Mesh will use. Each vertex is initialized to {0,0, 0,0, 1,1,1,1}.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How the vertices are used when drawing. The default mode 'fan' is sufficient for simple convex polygons.

arguments.name: usage
arguments.description: The expected usage of the Mesh. The specified usage mode affects the Mesh's memory usage and performance.

returns.name: mesh
returns.description: The new mesh.

arguments.name: vertexformat
arguments.description: A table in the form of {attribute, ...}. Each attribute is a table which specifies a custom vertex attribute used for each vertex.

table.name: attribute
table.description: A table containing the attribute's name, it's data type, and the number of components in the attribute, in the form of {name, datatype, components}.
Additional vertex attribute format tables.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: The table filled with vertex information tables for each vertex, in the form of {vertex, ...} where each vertex is a table in the form of {attributecomponent, ...}.

table.name: attributecomponent
table.description: The first component of the first vertex attribute in the vertex.
Additional components of all vertex attributes in the vertex.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How the vertices are used when drawing. The default mode 'fan' is sufficient for simple convex polygons.

arguments.name: usage
arguments.description: The expected usage of the Mesh. The specified usage mode affects the Mesh's memory usage and performance.

returns.name: mesh
returns.description: The new mesh.

variants.description:
Creates a Mesh with custom vertex attributes and the specified number of vertices.

Each vertex attribute component is initialized to 0 if its data type is 'float', or 1 if its data type is 'byte'. Vertex Shader is required in order to make use of the vertex attribute when the Mesh is drawn.

A Mesh '''must''' have a 'VertexPosition' attribute in order to be drawn, but it can be attached from a different Mesh via Mesh:attachAttribute.

entries.name: newQuad
entries.description:
Creates a new Quad.

The purpose of a Quad is to use a fraction of an image to draw objects, as opposed to drawing entire image. It is most useful for sprite sheets and atlases: in a sprite atlas, multiple sprites reside in same image, quad is used to draw a specific sprite from that image; in animated sprites with all frames residing in the same image, quad is used to draw specific frame from the animation.

arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the y-axis.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the Quad in the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: The height of the Quad in the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: sw
arguments.description: The reference width, the width of the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

arguments.name: sh
arguments.description: The reference height, the height of the Image. (Must be greater than 0.)

returns.name: quad
returns.description: The new Quad.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The top-left position in the Image along the y-axis.

arguments.name: height

arguments.description: The texture whose width and height will be used as the reference width and height.

returns.name: quad

entries.name: newShader
entries.description:
Creates a new Shader object for hardware-accelerated vertex and pixel effects. A Shader contains either vertex shader code, pixel shader code, or both.

Shaders are small programs which are run on the graphics card when drawing. Vertex shaders are run once for each vertex (for example, an image has 4 vertices - one at each corner. A Mesh might have many more.) Pixel shaders are run once for each pixel on the screen which the drawn object touches. Pixel shader code is executed after all the object's vertices have been processed by the vertex shader.

arguments.name: code
arguments.description: The pixel shader or vertex shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

returns.name: shader
returns.description: A Shader object for use in drawing operations.

arguments.name: pixelcode
arguments.description: The pixel shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

arguments.name: vertexcode
arguments.description: The vertex shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

returns.name: shader
returns.description: A Shader object for use in drawing operations.

entries.name: newVolumeImage
entries.description:
Creates a new volume (3D) Image.

Volume images are 3D textures with width, height, and depth. They can't be rendered directly, they can only be used in Shader code (and sent to the shader via Shader:send).

To use a volume image in a Shader, it must be declared as a VolumeImage or sampler3D type (instead of Image or sampler2D). The Texel(VolumeImage image, vec3 texcoords) shader function must be used to get pixel colors from the volume image. The vec3 argument is a normalized texture coordinate with the z component representing the depth to sample at (ranging from 1).

Volume images are typically used as lookup tables in shaders for color grading, for example, because sampling using a texture coordinate that is partway in between two pixels can interpolate across all 3 dimensions in the volume image, resulting in a smooth gradient even when a small-sized volume image is used as the lookup table.

Array images are a much better choice than volume images for storing multiple different sprites in a single array image for directly drawing them.

variants.description:
Creates a volume Image given multiple image files with matching dimensions.

Volume images are not supported on some older mobile devices. Use love.graphics.getTextureTypes to check at runtime.

arguments.name: layers
arguments.description: A table containing filepaths to images (or File, FileData, ImageData, or CompressedImageData objects), in an array. A table of tables can also be given, where each sub-table represents a single mipmap level and contains all layers for that mipmap.

arguments.name: settings
arguments.description: Optional table of settings to configure the volume image, containing the following fields:

table.name: mipmaps
table.description: True to make the image use mipmaps, false to disable them. Mipmaps will be automatically generated if the image isn't a compressed texture format.

table.name: linear
table.description: True to treat the image's pixels as linear instead of sRGB, when gamma correct rendering is enabled. Most images are authored as sRGB.

returns.name: image
returns.description: A volume Image object.

entries.name: origin
entries.description:
Resets the current coordinate transformation.

This function is always used to reverse any previous calls to love.graphics.rotate, love.graphics.scale, love.graphics.shear or love.graphics.translate. It returns the current transformation state to its defaults.

entries.name: points
entries.description: Draws one or more points.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The position of the first point on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The position of the first point on the y-axis.
The x and y coordinates of additional points.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A table containing multiple point positions, in the form of {x, y, ...}.

variants.description:
Draws one or more individually colored points.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

The pixel grid is actually offset to the center of each pixel. So to get clean pixels drawn use 0.5 + integer increments.

Points are not affected by size is always in pixels.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A table containing multiple individually colored points, in the form of {point, ...}.

table.name: point
table.description: A table containing the position and color of the first point, in the form of {x, y, r, g, b, a}. The color components are optional.
Additional tables containing the position and color of more points, in the form of {x, y, r, g, b, a}. The color components are optional.

entries.name: polygon
entries.description:
Draw a polygon.

Following the mode argument, this function can accept multiple numeric arguments or a single table of numeric arguments. In either case the arguments are interpreted as alternating x and y coordinates of the polygon's vertices.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How to draw the polygon.
The vertices of the polygon.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How to draw the polygon.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: The vertices of the polygon as a table.

entries.name: pop
entries.description:
Pops the current coordinate transformation from the transformation stack.

This function is always used to reverse a previous push operation. It returns the current transformation state to what it was before the last preceding push.

entries.name: present
entries.description:
Displays the results of drawing operations on the screen.

This function is used when writing your own love.run function. It presents all the results of your drawing operations on the screen. See the example in love.run for a typical use of this function.

variants.description: * If love.window.setMode has vsync equal to true, this function can't run more frequently than the refresh rate (e.g. 60 Hz), and will halt the program until ready if necessary.

entries.name: print
entries.description:
Draws text on screen. If no Font is set, one will be created and set (once) if needed.

As of LOVE 0.7.1, when using translation and scaling functions while drawing text, this function assumes the scale occurs first.  If you don't script with this in mind, the text won't be in the right position, or possibly even on screen.

love.graphics.print and love.graphics.printf both support UTF-8 encoding. You'll also need a proper Font for special characters.

In versions prior to 11.0, color and byte component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

entries.name: printf
entries.description:
Draws formatted text, with word wrap and alignment.

See additional notes in love.graphics.print.

The word wrap limit is applied before any scaling, rotation, and other coordinate transformations. Therefore the amount of text per line stays constant given the same wrap limit, even if the scale arguments change.

In version 0.9.2 and earlier, wrapping was implemented by breaking up words by spaces and putting them back together to make sure things fit nicely within the limit provided. However, due to the way this is done, extra spaces between words would end up missing when printed on the screen, and some lines could overflow past the provided wrap limit. In version 0.10.0 and newer this is no longer the case.

In versions prior to 11.0, color and byte component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

entries.name: push
entries.description:
Copies and pushes the current coordinate transformation to the transformation stack.

This function is always used to prepare for a corresponding pop operation later. It stores the current coordinate transformation state into the transformation stack and keeps it active. Later changes to the transformation can be undone by using the pop operation, which returns the coordinate transform to the state it was in before calling push.

variants.description: Pushes the current transformation to the transformation stack.
Pushes a specific type of state to the stack.

arguments.name: stack
arguments.description: The type of stack to push (e.g. just transformation state, or all love.graphics state).

entries.name: rectangle
entries.description: Draws a rectangle.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How to draw the rectangle.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The position of top-left corner along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The position of top-left corner along the y-axis.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: Width of the rectangle.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: Height of the rectangle.

variants.description: Draws a rectangle with rounded corners.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: How to draw the rectangle.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The position of top-left corner along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The position of top-left corner along the y-axis.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: Width of the rectangle.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: Height of the rectangle.

arguments.name: rx
arguments.description: The x-axis radius of each round corner. Cannot be greater than half the rectangle's width.

arguments.name: ry
arguments.description: The y-axis radius of each round corner. Cannot be greater than half the rectangle's height.

arguments.name: segments
arguments.description: The number of segments used for drawing the round corners. A default amount will be chosen if no number is given.

entries.name: replaceTransform
entries.description: Replaces the current coordinate transformation with the given Transform object.

arguments.name: transform
arguments.description: The Transform object to replace the current graphics coordinate transform with.

entries.name: reset
entries.description:
Resets the current graphics settings.

Calling reset makes the current drawing color white, the current background color black, disables any active color component masks, disables wireframe mode and resets the current graphics transformation to the origin. It also sets both the point and line drawing modes to smooth and their sizes to 1.0.

entries.name: rotate
entries.description:
Rotates the coordinate system in two dimensions.

Calling this function affects all future drawing operations by rotating the coordinate system around the origin by the given amount of radians. This change lasts until love.draw() exits.

arguments.name: angle
arguments.description: The amount to rotate the coordinate system in radians.

entries.name: scale
entries.description:
Scales the coordinate system in two dimensions.

By default the coordinate system in LÖVE corresponds to the display pixels in horizontal and vertical directions one-to-one, and the x-axis increases towards the right while the y-axis increases downwards. Scaling the coordinate system changes this relation.

After scaling by sx and sy, all coordinates are treated as if they were multiplied by sx and sy. Every result of a drawing operation is also correspondingly scaled, so scaling by (2, 2) for example would mean making everything twice as large in both x- and y-directions. Scaling by a negative value flips the coordinate system in the corresponding direction, which also means everything will be drawn flipped or upside down, or both. Scaling by zero is not a useful operation.

Scale and translate are not commutative operations, therefore, calling them in different orders will change the outcome.

Scaling lasts until love.draw() exits.

arguments.name: sx
arguments.description: The scaling in the direction of the x-axis.

arguments.name: sy
arguments.description: The scaling in the direction of the y-axis. If omitted, it defaults to same as parameter sx.

entries.name: setBackgroundColor
entries.description: Sets the background color.

arguments.name: red
arguments.description: The red component (0-1).

arguments.name: green
arguments.description: The green component (0-1).

arguments.name: blue
arguments.description: The blue component (0-1).

arguments.name: alpha
arguments.description: The alpha component (0-1).

arguments.name: rgba
arguments.description: A numerical indexed table with the red, green, blue and alpha values as numbers. The alpha is optional and defaults to 1 if it is left out.

entries.name: setBlendMode
entries.description: Sets the blending mode.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: The blend mode to use.

variants.description: The default 'alphamultiply' alpha mode should normally be preferred except when drawing content with pre-multiplied alpha. If content is drawn to a Canvas using the 'alphamultiply' mode, the Canvas texture will have pre-multiplied alpha afterwards, so the 'premultiplied' alpha mode should generally be used when drawing a Canvas to the screen.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: The blend mode to use.

arguments.name: alphamode
arguments.description: What to do with the alpha of drawn objects when blending.

entries.name: setCanvas
entries.description: Captures drawing operations to a Canvas.

variants.description:
Sets the render target to a specified stencil or depth testing with an active Canvas, the stencil buffer or depth buffer must be explicitly enabled in setCanvas via the variants below.

Note that no canvas should be active when ''love.graphics.present'' is called. ''love.graphics.present'' is called at the end of love.draw in the default love.run, hence if you activate a canvas using this function, you normally need to deactivate it at some point before ''love.draw'' finishes.

arguments.name: canvas
arguments.description: The new target.

arguments.name: mipmap
arguments.description: The mipmap level to render to, for Canvases with mipmaps.

variants.description: Resets the render target to the screen, i.e. re-enables drawing to the screen.

Sets the render target to multiple simultaneous 2D Canvases. All drawing operations until the next ''love.graphics.setCanvas'' call will be redirected to the specified canvases and not shown on the screen.

Normally all drawing operations will draw only to the first canvas passed to the function, but that can be changed if a pixel shader is used with the void effect function instead of the regular vec4 effect.

All canvas arguments must have the same widths and heights and the same texture type. Not all computers which support Canvases will support multiple render targets. If love.graphics.isSupported('multicanvas') returns true, at least 4 simultaneously active canvases are supported.

arguments.name: canvas1
arguments.description: The first render target.

arguments.name: canvas2
arguments.description: The second render target.
More canvases.

variants.description: Sets the render target to the specified layer/slice and mipmap level of the given non-2D Canvas. All drawing operations until the next ''love.graphics.setCanvas'' call will be redirected to the Canvas and not shown on the screen.

arguments.name: canvas
arguments.description: The new render target.

arguments.name: slice
arguments.description: For cubemaps this is the cube face index to render to (between 1 and 6). For Array textures this is the array layer. For volume textures this is the depth slice. 2D canvases should use a value of 1.

arguments.name: mipmap
arguments.description: The mipmap level to render to, for Canvases with mipmaps.

arguments.name: setup
arguments.description: A table specifying the active Canvas(es), their mipmap levels and active layers if applicable, and whether to use a stencil and/or depth buffer.

table.description: The Canvas to render to.
An additional Canvas to render to, if multiple simultaneous render targets are wanted.
Additional Canvases to render to, if multiple simultaneous render targets are wanted.

table.name: stencil
table.description: Whether an internally managed stencil buffer should be used, if the depthstencil field isn't set.

table.name: depth
table.description: Whether an internally managed depth buffer should be used, if the depthstencil field isn't set.

table.name: depthstencil
table.description: An optional custom depth/stencil formatted Canvas to use for the depth and/or stencil buffer.

entries.name: setColor
entries.description:
Sets the color used for drawing.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

arguments.name: red
arguments.description: The amount of red.

arguments.name: green
arguments.description: The amount of green.

arguments.name: blue
arguments.description: The amount of blue.

arguments.name: alpha
arguments.description: The amount of alpha.  The alpha value will be applied to all subsequent draw operations, even the drawing of an image.

arguments.name: rgba
arguments.description: A numerical indexed table with the red, green, blue and alpha values as numbers. The alpha is optional and defaults to 1 if it is left out.

entries.name: setColorMask
entries.description: Sets the color mask. Enables or disables specific color components when rendering and clearing the screen. For example, if '''red''' is set to '''false''', no further changes will be made to the red component of any pixels.

variants.description: Enables color masking for the specified color components.

arguments.name: red
arguments.description: Render red component.

arguments.name: green
arguments.description: Render green component.

arguments.name: blue
arguments.description: Render blue component.

arguments.name: alpha
arguments.description: Render alpha component.

variants.description: Disables color masking.

entries.name: setDefaultFilter
entries.description: Sets the default scaling filters used with Images, Canvases, and Fonts.

variants.description: This function does not apply retroactively to loaded images.

arguments.name: min
arguments.description: Filter mode used when scaling the image down.

arguments.name: mag
arguments.description: Filter mode used when scaling the image up.

arguments.name: anisotropy
arguments.description: Maximum amount of Anisotropic Filtering used.

entries.name: setDepthMode
entries.description:
Configures depth testing and writing to the depth buffer.

This is low-level functionality designed for use with custom vertex shaders and Meshes with custom vertex attributes. No higher level APIs are provided to set the depth of 2D graphics such as shapes, lines, and Images.

arguments.name: comparemode
arguments.description: Depth comparison mode used for depth testing.

arguments.name: write
arguments.description: Whether to write update / write values to the depth buffer when rendering.

variants.description: Disables depth testing and depth writes.

entries.name: setFont
entries.description:
Set an already-loaded Font as the current font or create and load a new one from the file and size.

It's recommended that Font objects are created with love.graphics.newFont in the loading stage and then passed to this function in the drawing stage.

arguments.name: font
arguments.description: The Font object to use.

entries.name: setFrontFaceWinding
entries.description:
Sets whether triangles with clockwise- or counterclockwise-ordered vertices are considered front-facing.

This is designed for use in combination with Mesh face culling. Other love.graphics shapes, lines, and sprites are not guaranteed to have a specific winding order to their internal vertices.

arguments.name: winding
arguments.description: The winding mode to use. The default winding is counterclockwise ('ccw').

entries.name: setLineJoin
entries.description: Sets the line join style. See LineJoin for the possible options.

arguments.name: join
arguments.description: The LineJoin to use.

entries.name: setLineStyle
entries.description: Sets the line style.

arguments.name: style
arguments.description: The LineStyle to use. Line styles include smooth and rough.

entries.name: setLineWidth
entries.description: Sets the line width.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: The width of the line.

entries.name: setMeshCullMode
entries.description:
Sets whether back-facing triangles in a Mesh are culled.

This is designed for use with low level custom hardware-accelerated 3D rendering via custom vertex attributes on Meshes, custom vertex shaders, and depth testing with a depth buffer.

By default, both front- and back-facing triangles in Meshes are rendered.

arguments.name: mode
arguments.description: The Mesh face culling mode to use (whether to render everything, cull back-facing triangles, or cull front-facing triangles).

entries.description: Creates and sets a new Font.

entries.name: setScissor
entries.description:
Sets or disables scissor.

The scissor limits the drawing area to a specified rectangle. This affects all graphics calls, including love.graphics.clear. 

The dimensions of the scissor is unaffected by graphical transformations (translate, scale, ...).

variants.description: Limits the drawing area to a specified rectangle.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: x coordinate of upper left corner.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: y coordinate of upper left corner.

arguments.name: width
arguments.description: width of clipping rectangle.

arguments.name: height
arguments.description: height of clipping rectangle.

variants.description: Disables scissor.

entries.name: setShader
entries.description: Sets or resets a Shader as the current pixel effect or vertex shaders. All drawing operations until the next ''love.graphics.setShader'' will be drawn using the Shader object specified.

variants.description: Sets the current shader to a specified Shader. All drawing operations until the next ''love.graphics.setShader'' will be drawn using the Shader object specified.

arguments.name: shader
arguments.description: The new shader.

variants.description: Disables shaders, allowing unfiltered drawing operations.

entries.name: setStencilTest
entries.description:
Configures or disables stencil testing.

When stencil testing is enabled, the geometry of everything that is drawn afterward will be clipped / stencilled out based on a comparison between the arguments of this function and the stencil value of each pixel that the geometry touches. The stencil values of pixels are affected via love.graphics.stencil.

arguments.name: comparemode
arguments.description: The type of comparison to make for each pixel.

arguments.name: comparevalue
arguments.description: The value to use when comparing with the stencil value of each pixel. Must be between 0 and 255.

variants.description: Disables stencil testing.

entries.name: setWireframe
entries.description: Sets whether wireframe lines will be used when drawing.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: True to enable wireframe mode when drawing, false to disable it.

entries.name: shear
entries.description: Shears the coordinate system.

arguments.name: kx
arguments.description: The shear factor on the x-axis.

arguments.name: ky
arguments.description: The shear factor on the y-axis.

entries.name: stencil
entries.description:
Draws geometry as a stencil.

The geometry drawn by the supplied function sets invisible stencil values of pixels, instead of setting pixel colors. The stencil buffer (which contains those stencil values) can act like a mask / stencil - love.graphics.setStencilTest can be used afterward to determine how further rendering is affected by the stencil values in each pixel.

Stencil values are integers within the range of 255.

variants.description: It is possible to draw to the screen and to the stencil values of pixels at the same time, by using love.graphics.setColorMask inside the stencil function to enable drawing to all color components.

arguments.name: stencilfunction
arguments.description: Function which draws geometry. The stencil values of pixels, rather than the color of each pixel, will be affected by the geometry.

arguments.name: action
arguments.description: How to modify any stencil values of pixels that are touched by what's drawn in the stencil function.

arguments.name: value
arguments.description: The new stencil value to use for pixels if the 'replace' stencil action is used. Has no effect with other stencil actions. Must be between 0 and 255.

arguments.name: keepvalues
arguments.description: True to preserve old stencil values of pixels, false to re-set every pixel's stencil value to 0 before executing the stencil function. love.graphics.clear will also re-set all stencil values.

entries.name: transformPoint
entries.description:
Converts the given 2D position from global coordinates into screen-space.

This effectively applies the current graphics transformations to the given position. A similar Transform:transformPoint method exists for Transform objects.

arguments.name: globalX
arguments.description: The x component of the position in global coordinates.

arguments.name: globalY
arguments.description: The y component of the position in global coordinates.

returns.name: screenX
returns.description: The x component of the position with graphics transformations applied.

returns.name: screenY
returns.description: The y component of the position with graphics transformations applied.

entries.name: translate
entries.description:
Translates the coordinate system in two dimensions.

When this function is called with two numbers, dx, and dy, all the following drawing operations take effect as if their x and y coordinates were x+dx and y+dy. 

Scale and translate are not commutative operations, therefore, calling them in different orders will change the outcome.

This change lasts until love.draw() exits or else a love.graphics.pop reverts to a previous love.graphics.push.

Translating using whole numbers will prevent tearing/blurring of images and fonts draw after translating.

arguments.name: dx
arguments.description: The translation relative to the x-axis.

arguments.name: dy
arguments.description: The translation relative to the y-axis.

entries.name: validateShader
entries.description: Validates shader code. Check if specified shader code does not contain any errors.

arguments.name: gles
arguments.description: Validate code as GLSL ES shader.

arguments.name: code
arguments.description: The pixel shader or vertex shader code, or a filename pointing to a file with the code.

returns.name: status
returns.description: true if specified shader code doesn't contain any errors. false otherwise.

returns.name: message
returns.description: Reason why shader code validation failed (or nil if validation succeded).

arguments.name: gles

arguments.name: pixelcode

arguments.name: vertexcode

returns.name: status
returns.description: true if specified shader code doesn't contain any errors. false otherwise.

returns.name: message
returns.description: Reason why shader code validation failed (or nil if validation succeded).

entries.name: hasDeprecationOutput
entries.description:
Gets whether LÖVE displays warnings when using deprecated functionality. It is disabled by default in fused mode, and enabled by default otherwise.

When deprecation output is enabled, the first use of a formally deprecated LÖVE API will show a message at the bottom of the screen for a short time, and print the message to the console.

returns.name: enabled
returns.description: Whether deprecation output is enabled.

entries.name: isVersionCompatible
entries.description: Gets whether the given version is compatible with the current running version of LÖVE.

arguments.name: version
arguments.description: The version to check (for example '11.3' or '0.10.2').

returns.name: compatible
returns.description: Whether the given version is compatible with the current running version of LÖVE.

arguments.description: The major version to check (for example 11 for 11.3 or 0 for 0.10.2).

arguments.name: minor
arguments.description: The minor version to check (for example 3 for 11.3 or 10 for 0.10.2).

arguments.name: revision
arguments.description: The revision of version to check (for example 0 for 11.3 or 2 for 0.10.2).

returns.name: compatible
returns.description: Whether the given version is compatible with the current running version of LÖVE.

entries.name: getGamepadMappingString
entries.description:
Gets the full gamepad mapping string of the Joysticks which have the given GUID, or nil if the GUID isn't recognized as a gamepad.

The mapping string contains binding information used to map the Joystick's buttons an axes to the standard gamepad layout, and can be used later with love.joystick.loadGamepadMappings.

arguments.name: guid
arguments.description: The GUID value to get the mapping string for.

returns.name: mappingstring
returns.description: A string containing the Joystick's gamepad mappings, or nil if the GUID is not recognized as a gamepad.

entries.name: getJoystickCount
entries.description: Gets the number of connected joysticks.

returns.name: joystickcount
returns.description: The number of connected joysticks.

entries.name: getJoysticks
entries.description: Gets a list of connected Joysticks.

returns.name: joysticks
returns.description: The list of currently connected Joysticks.

entries.name: loadGamepadMappings
entries.description:
Loads a gamepad mappings string or file created with love.joystick.saveGamepadMappings.

It also recognizes any SDL gamecontroller mapping string, such as those created with Steam's Big Picture controller configure interface, or this nice database. If a new mapping is loaded for an already known controller GUID, the later version will overwrite the one currently loaded.

variants.description: Loads a gamepad mappings string from a file.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filename to load the mappings string from.

variants.description: Loads a gamepad mappings string directly.

arguments.name: mappings
arguments.description: The mappings string to load.

entries.name: saveGamepadMappings
entries.description:
Saves the virtual gamepad mappings of all recognized as gamepads and have either been recently used or their gamepad bindings have been modified.

The mappings are stored as a string for use with love.joystick.loadGamepadMappings.

variants.description: Saves the gamepad mappings of all relevant joysticks to a file.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: The filename to save the mappings string to.

returns.name: mappings
returns.description: The mappings string that was written to the file.

variants.description: Returns the mappings string without writing to a file.

returns.name: mappings
returns.description: The mappings string.

entries.name: setGamepadMapping
entries.description:
Binds a virtual gamepad input to a button, axis or hat for all Joysticks of a certain type. For example, if this function is used with a GUID returned by a Dualshock 3 controller in OS X, the binding will affect Joystick:getGamepadAxis and Joystick:isGamepadDown for ''all'' Dualshock 3 controllers used with the game when run in OS X.

LÖVE includes built-in gamepad bindings for many common controllers. This function lets you change the bindings or add new ones for types of Joysticks which aren't recognized as gamepads by default.

The virtual gamepad buttons and axes are designed around the Xbox 360 controller layout.

arguments.name: guid
arguments.description: The OS-dependent GUID for the type of Joystick the binding will affect.

arguments.name: button
arguments.description: The virtual gamepad button to bind.

arguments.name: inputtype
arguments.description: The type of input to bind the virtual gamepad button to.

arguments.name: inputindex
arguments.description: The index of the axis, button, or hat to bind the virtual gamepad button to.

arguments.name: hatdir
arguments.description: The direction of the hat, if the virtual gamepad button will be bound to a hat. nil otherwise.

returns.name: success
returns.description: Whether the virtual gamepad button was successfully bound.

variants.description: The physical locations for the bound gamepad axes and buttons should correspond as closely as possible to the layout of a standard Xbox 360 controller.

entries.name: getKeyFromScancode
entries.description:
Gets the key corresponding to the given hardware scancode.

Unlike key constants, Scancodes are keyboard layout-independent. For example the scancode 'w' will be generated if the key in the same place as the 'w' key on an American keyboard is pressed, no matter what the key is labelled or what the user's operating system settings are.

Scancodes are useful for creating default controls that have the same physical locations on on all systems.

arguments.name: scancode
arguments.description: The scancode to get the key from.

returns.name: key
returns.description: The key corresponding to the given scancode, or 'unknown' if the scancode doesn't map to a KeyConstant on the current system.

entries.name: getScancodeFromKey
entries.description:
Gets the hardware scancode corresponding to the given key.

Unlike key constants, Scancodes are keyboard layout-independent. For example the scancode 'w' will be generated if the key in the same place as the 'w' key on an American keyboard is pressed, no matter what the key is labelled or what the user's operating system settings are.

Scancodes are useful for creating default controls that have the same physical locations on on all systems.

arguments.name: key
arguments.description: The key to get the scancode from.

returns.name: scancode
returns.description: The scancode corresponding to the given key, or 'unknown' if the given key has no known physical representation on the current system.

Enables or disables text input events. It is enabled by default on Windows, Mac, and Linux, and disabled by default on iOS and Android.

On touch devices, this shows the system's native on-screen keyboard when it's enabled.

entries.name: lowmemory
entries.description:
Callback function triggered when the system is running out of memory on mobile devices.

Mobile operating systems may forcefully kill the game if it uses too much memory, so any non-critical resource should be removed if possible (by setting all variables referencing the resources to '''nil'''), when this event is triggered. Sounds and images in particular tend to use the most memory.

entries.name: colorFromBytes
entries.description: Converts a color from 0..255 to 0..1 range.

variants.description:
Here's implementation for 11.2 and earlier.

function love.math.colorFromBytes(r, g, b, a)

	if type(r) == 'table' then

		r, g, b, a = rr[2, rr[4

	end

	r = clamp01(floor(r + 0.5) / 255)

	g = clamp01(floor(g + 0.5) / 255)

	b = clamp01(floor(b + 0.5) / 255)

	a = a ~= nil and clamp01(floor(a + 0.5) / 255) or nil

	return r, g, b, a

end

Where clamp01 is defined as follows

local function clamp01(x)

	return math.min(math.max(x, 0), 1)

end

arguments.name: rb
arguments.description: Red color component in 0..255 range.

arguments.name: gb
arguments.description: Green color component in 0..255 range.

arguments.name: bb
arguments.description: Blue color component in 0..255 range.

arguments.name: ab
arguments.description: Alpha color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: r
returns.description: Red color component in 0..1 range.

returns.name: g
returns.description: Green color component in 0..1 range.

returns.name: b
returns.description: Blue color component in 0..1 range.

returns.name: a
returns.description: Alpha color component in 0..1 range or nil if alpha is not specified.

entries.name: colorToBytes
entries.description: Converts a color from 0..1 to 0..255 range.

variants.description:
Here's implementation for 11.2 and earlier.

function love.math.colorToBytes(r, g, b, a)

	if type(r) == 'table' then

		r, g, b, a = rr[2, rr[4

	end

	r = floor(clamp01(r) * 255 + 0.5)

	g = floor(clamp01(g) * 255 + 0.5)

	b = floor(clamp01(b) * 255 + 0.5)

	a = a ~= nil and floor(clamp01(a) * 255 + 0.5) or nil

	return r, g, b, a

end

Where clamp01 is defined as follows

local function clamp01(x)

	return math.min(math.max(x, 0), 1)

end

arguments.name: r
arguments.description: Red color component.

arguments.name: g
arguments.description: Green color component.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: Blue color component.

arguments.name: a
arguments.description: Alpha color component.

returns.name: gb
returns.description: Green color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: bb
returns.description: Blue color component in 0..255 range.

returns.name: ab
returns.description: Alpha color component in 0..255 range or nil if alpha is not specified.

entries.name: gammaToLinear
entries.description:
Converts a color from gamma-space (sRGB) to linear-space (RGB). This is useful when doing gamma-correct rendering and you need to do math in linear RGB in the few cases where LÖVE doesn't handle conversions automatically.

Read more about gamma-correct rendering here, here, and here.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

variants.description: An alpha value can be passed into the function as a fourth argument, but it will be returned unchanged because alpha is always linear.

arguments.name: r
arguments.description: The red channel of the sRGB color to convert.

arguments.name: g
arguments.description: The green channel of the sRGB color to convert.

arguments.name: b
arguments.description: The blue channel of the sRGB color to convert.

returns.name: lr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

arguments.name: color
arguments.description: An array with the red, green, and blue channels of the sRGB color to convert.

returns.name: lr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

returns.name: lb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in linear RGB space.

arguments.name: c
arguments.description: The value of a color channel in sRGB space to convert.

returns.name: lc
returns.description: The value of the color channel in linear RGB space.

entries.name: getRandomSeed
entries.description:
Gets the seed of the random number generator.

The seed is split into two numbers due to Lua's use of doubles for all number values - doubles can't accurately represent integer  values above 2^53, but the seed can be an integer value up to 2^64.

returns.name: low
returns.description: Integer number representing the lower 32 bits of the random number generator's 64 bit seed value.

returns.name: high
returns.description: Integer number representing the higher 32 bits of the random number generator's 64 bit seed value.

entries.name: getRandomState
entries.description:
Gets the current state of the random number generator. This returns an opaque implementation-dependent string which is only useful for later use with love.math.setRandomState or RandomGenerator:setState.

This is different from love.math.getRandomSeed in that getRandomState gets the random number generator's current state, whereas getRandomSeed gets the previously set seed number.

variants.description: The value of the state string does not depend on the current operating system.

returns.name: state
returns.description: The current state of the random number generator, represented as a string.

entries.name: isConvex

Checks whether a polygon is convex.

PolygonShapes in love.physics, some forms of Meshes, and polygons drawn with love.graphics.polygon must be simple convex polygons.

arguments.name: y2

Additional position of the vertex of the polygon on the x-axis and y-axis.

entries.name: linearToGamma
entries.description:
Converts a color from linear-space (RGB) to gamma-space (sRGB). This is useful when storing linear RGB color values in an image, because the linear RGB color space has less precision than sRGB for dark colors, which can result in noticeable color banding when drawing.

In general, colors chosen based on what they look like on-screen are already in gamma-space and should not be double-converted. Colors calculated using math are often in the linear RGB space.

Read more about gamma-correct rendering here, here, and here.

In versions prior to 11.0, color component values were within the range of 0 to 255 instead of 0 to 1.

variants.description: An alpha value can be passed into the function as a fourth argument, but it will be returned unchanged because alpha is always linear.

arguments.name: lr
arguments.description: The red channel of the linear RGB color to convert.

arguments.name: lg
arguments.description: The green channel of the linear RGB color to convert.

arguments.name: lb
arguments.description: The blue channel of the linear RGB color to convert.

returns.name: cr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

arguments.name: color
arguments.description: An array with the red, green, and blue channels of the linear RGB color to convert.

returns.name: cr
returns.description: The red channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cg
returns.description: The green channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

returns.name: cb
returns.description: The blue channel of the converted color in gamma sRGB space.

arguments.name: lc
arguments.description: The value of a color channel in linear RGB space to convert.

returns.name: c
returns.description: The value of the color channel in gamma sRGB space.
