Creates a new BezierCurve object.

The number of vertices in the control polygon determines the degree of the curve, e.g. three vertices define a quadratic (degree 2) Bézier curve, four vertices define a cubic (degree 3) Bézier curve, etc.

entries.name: noise
entries.description:
Generates a Simplex or Perlin noise value in 1-4 dimensions. The return value will always be the same, given the same arguments.

Simplex noise is closely related to Perlin noise. It is widely used for procedural content generation.

There are many webpages which discuss Perlin and Simplex noise in detail.

variants.description: Generates Simplex noise from 1 dimension.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The number used to generate the noise value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

variants.description: Generates Simplex noise from 2 dimensions.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The first value of the 2-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The second value of the 2-dimensional vector used to generate the noise value.

returns.name: value
returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

arguments.name: y

arguments.name: z

returns.description: The noise value in the range of 1.

entries.name: setRandomSeed
entries.description: Sets the seed of the random number generator using the specified integer number. This is called internally at startup, so you generally don't need to call it yourself.

variants.description: Due to Lua's use of double-precision floating point numbers, integer values above 2^53 cannot be accurately represented. Use the other variant of the function if you want to use a larger number.

arguments.name: seed
arguments.description: The integer number with which you want to seed the randomization. Must be within the range of 2^53 - 1.

variants.description: Combines two 32-bit integer numbers into a 64-bit integer value and sets the seed of the random number generator using the value.

arguments.name: low
arguments.description: The lower 32 bits of the seed value. Must be within the range of 2^32 - 1.

arguments.name: high
arguments.description: The higher 32 bits of the seed value. Must be within the range of 2^32 - 1.

entries.name: setRandomState
entries.description:
Sets the current state of the random number generator. The value used as an argument for this function is an opaque implementation-dependent string and should only originate from a previous call to love.math.getRandomState.

This is different from love.math.setRandomSeed in that setRandomState directly sets the random number generator's current implementation-dependent state, whereas setRandomSeed gives it a new seed value.

variants.description: The effect of the state string does not depend on the current operating system.

arguments.name: state
arguments.description: The new state of the random number generator, represented as a string. This should originate from a previous call to love.math.getRandomState.

entries.name: getCursor
entries.description: Gets the current Cursor.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The current cursor, or nil if no cursor is set.

entries.name: getPosition
entries.description: Returns the current position of the mouse.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the x-axis.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the y-axis.

entries.name: getRelativeMode
entries.description:
Gets whether relative mode is enabled for the mouse.

If relative mode is enabled, the cursor is hidden and doesn't move when the mouse does, but relative mouse motion events are still generated via love.mousemoved. This lets the mouse move in any direction indefinitely without the cursor getting stuck at the edges of the screen.

The reported position of the mouse is not updated while relative mode is enabled, even when relative mouse motion events are generated.

returns.name: enabled
returns.description: True if relative mode is enabled, false if it's disabled.

entries.name: getSystemCursor
entries.description:
Gets a Cursor object representing a system-native hardware cursor.

Hardware cursors are framerate-independent and work the same way as normal operating system cursors. Unlike drawing an image at the mouse's current coordinates, hardware cursors never have visible lag between when the mouse is moved and when the cursor position updates, even at low framerates.

variants.description: The 'image' CursorType is not a valid argument. Use love.mouse.newCursor to create a hardware cursor using a custom image.

arguments.name: ctype
arguments.description: The type of system cursor to get.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The Cursor object representing the system cursor type.

entries.name: getX
entries.description: Returns the current x-position of the mouse.

returns.name: x
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the x-axis.

entries.name: getY
entries.description: Returns the current y-position of the mouse.

returns.name: y
returns.description: The position of the mouse along the y-axis.

entries.name: isCursorSupported
entries.description:
Gets whether cursor functionality is supported.

If it isn't supported, calling love.mouse.newCursor and love.mouse.getSystemCursor will cause an error. Mobile devices do not support cursors.

returns.name: supported
returns.description: Whether the system has cursor functionality.

entries.name: isDown
entries.description:
Checks whether a certain mouse button is down.

This function does not detect mouse wheel scrolling; you must use the love.wheelmoved (or love.mousepressed in version 0.9.2 and older) callback for that.

arguments.name: button
arguments.description: The index of a button to check. 1 is the primary mouse button, 2 is the secondary mouse button and 3 is the middle button. Further buttons are mouse dependant.
Additional button numbers to check.

returns.name: down
returns.description: True if any specified button is down.

entries.name: isGrabbed
entries.description: Checks if the mouse is grabbed.

returns.name: grabbed
returns.description: True if the cursor is grabbed, false if it is not.

entries.name: isVisible
entries.description: Checks if the cursor is visible.

returns.name: visible
returns.description: True if the cursor to visible, false if the cursor is hidden.

entries.name: newCursor
entries.description:
Creates a new hardware Cursor object from an image file or ImageData.

Hardware cursors are framerate-independent and work the same way as normal operating system cursors. Unlike drawing an image at the mouse's current coordinates, hardware cursors never have visible lag between when the mouse is moved and when the cursor position updates, even at low framerates.

The hot spot is the point the operating system uses to determine what was clicked and at what position the mouse cursor is. For example, the normal arrow pointer normally has its hot spot at the top left of the image, but a crosshair cursor might have it in the middle.

arguments.name: imageData
arguments.description: The ImageData to use for the new Cursor.

arguments.name: hotx
arguments.description: The x-coordinate in the ImageData of the cursor's hot spot.

arguments.name: hoty
arguments.description: The y-coordinate in the ImageData of the cursor's hot spot.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The new Cursor object.

arguments.name: filename
arguments.description: Path to the image to use for the new Cursor.

arguments.name: hotx
arguments.description: The x-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

arguments.name: hoty
arguments.description: The y-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The new Cursor object.

arguments.name: fileData
arguments.description: Data representing the image to use for the new Cursor.

arguments.name: hotx
arguments.description: The x-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

arguments.name: hoty
arguments.description: The y-coordinate in the image of the cursor's hot spot.

returns.name: cursor
returns.description: The new Cursor object.

entries.name: setCursor
entries.description: Sets the current mouse cursor.

arguments.name: cursor
arguments.description: The Cursor object to use as the current mouse cursor.

variants.description: Resets the current mouse cursor to the default.

entries.name: setGrabbed
entries.description: Grabs the mouse and confines it to the window.

arguments.name: grab
arguments.description: True to confine the mouse, false to let it leave the window.

entries.name: setPosition
entries.description: Sets the current position of the mouse. Non-integer values are floored.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the y-axis.

entries.name: setRelativeMode
entries.description:
Sets whether relative mode is enabled for the mouse.

When relative mode is enabled, the cursor is hidden and doesn't move when the mouse does, but relative mouse motion events are still generated via love.mousemoved. This lets the mouse move in any direction indefinitely without the cursor getting stuck at the edges of the screen.

The reported position of the mouse may not be updated while relative mode is enabled, even when relative mouse motion events are generated.

arguments.name: enable
arguments.description: True to enable relative mode, false to disable it.

entries.name: setVisible
entries.description: Sets the current visibility of the cursor.

arguments.name: visible
arguments.description: True to set the cursor to visible, false to hide the cursor.

entries.name: setX
entries.description:
Sets the current X position of the mouse.

Non-integer values are floored.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the x-axis.

entries.name: setY
entries.description:
Sets the current Y position of the mouse.

Non-integer values are floored.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The new position of the mouse along the y-axis.

entries.name: mousefocus
entries.description: Callback function triggered when window receives or loses mouse focus.

arguments.name: focus
arguments.description: Whether the window has mouse focus or not.

entries.name: mousemoved
entries.description: Callback function triggered when the mouse is moved.

variants.description: If Relative Mode is enabled for the mouse, the '''dx''' and '''dy''' arguments of this callback will update but '''x''' and '''y''' are not guaranteed to.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The mouse position on the x-axis.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The mouse position on the y-axis.

arguments.name: dx
arguments.description: The amount moved along the x-axis since the last time love.mousemoved was called.

arguments.name: dy
arguments.description: The amount moved along the y-axis since the last time love.mousemoved was called.

arguments.name: istouch
arguments.description: True if the mouse button press originated from a touchscreen touch-press.

entries.name: mousepressed
entries.description: Callback function triggered when a mouse button is pressed.

variants.description: Use love.wheelmoved to detect mouse wheel motion. It will not register as a button press in version 0.10.0 and newer.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: Mouse x position, in pixels.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: Mouse y position, in pixels.

arguments.name: button
arguments.description: The button index that was pressed. 1 is the primary mouse button, 2 is the secondary mouse button and 3 is the middle button. Further buttons are mouse dependent.

arguments.name: istouch
arguments.description: True if the mouse button press originated from a touchscreen touch-press.

arguments.name: presses
arguments.description: The number of presses in a short time frame and small area, used to simulate double, triple clicks

entries.name: mousereleased
entries.description: Callback function triggered when a mouse button is released.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: Mouse x position, in pixels.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: Mouse y position, in pixels.

arguments.name: button
arguments.description: The button index that was released. 1 is the primary mouse button, 2 is the secondary mouse button and 3 is the middle button. Further buttons are mouse dependent.

arguments.name: istouch
arguments.description: True if the mouse button release originated from a touchscreen touch-release.

arguments.name: presses
arguments.description: The number of presses in a short time frame and small area, used to simulate double, triple clicks

entries.name: getDistance
entries.description: Returns the two closest points between two fixtures and their distance.

arguments.name: fixture1
arguments.description: The first fixture.

arguments.name: fixture2
arguments.description: The second fixture.

returns.name: distance
returns.description: The distance of the two points.

returns.name: x1
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the first point.

returns.name: y1
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the first point.

returns.name: x2
returns.description: The x-coordinate of the second point.

returns.name: y2
returns.description: The y-coordinate of the second point.

entries.name: getMeter
entries.description:
Returns the meter scale factor.

All coordinates in the physics module are divided by this number, creating a convenient way to draw the objects directly to the screen without the need for graphics transformations.

It is recommended to create shapes no larger than 10 times the scale. This is important because Box2D is tuned to work well with shape sizes from 0.1 to 10 meters.

returns.name: scale
returns.description: The scale factor as an integer.

entries.name: newBody
entries.description:
Creates a new body.

There are three types of bodies. 

* Static bodies do not move, have a infinite mass, and can be used for level boundaries. 

* Dynamic bodies are the main actors in the simulation, they collide with everything. 

* Kinematic bodies do not react to forces and only collide with dynamic bodies.

The mass of the body gets calculated when a Fixture is attached or removed, but can be changed at any time with Body:setMass or Body:resetMassData.

arguments.name: world
arguments.description: The world to create the body in.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the body.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the body.

arguments.name: type
arguments.description: The type of the body.

returns.name: body
returns.description: A new body.

entries.name: newChainShape
entries.description: Creates a new ChainShape.

arguments.name: loop
arguments.description: If the chain should loop back to the first point.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second point.
Additional point positions.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

arguments.name: loop
arguments.description: If the chain should loop back to the first point.

arguments.name: points
arguments.description: A list of points to construct the ChainShape, in the form of {x1, y1, x2, y2, ...}.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

entries.name: newCircleShape
entries.description: Creates a new CircleShape.

arguments.name: radius
arguments.description: The radius of the circle.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the circle.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the circle.

arguments.name: radius
arguments.description: The radius of the circle.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: The new shape.

entries.name: newDistanceJoint
entries.description:
Creates a DistanceJoint between two bodies.

This joint constrains the distance between two points on two bodies to be constant. These two points are specified in world coordinates and the two bodies are assumed to be in place when this joint is created. The first anchor point is connected to the first body and the second to the second body, and the points define the length of the distance joint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second anchor point (world space).

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new distance joint.

entries.name: newEdgeShape
entries.description: Creates a new EdgeShape.

arguments.name: y1

arguments.name: x2

arguments.name: y2

entries.name: newFixture
entries.description:
Creates and attaches a Fixture to a body.

Note that the Shape object is copied rather than kept as a reference when the Fixture is created. To get the Shape object that the Fixture owns, use Fixture:getShape.

arguments.name: body
arguments.description: The body which gets the fixture attached.

arguments.name: shape
arguments.description: The shape to be copied to the fixture.

arguments.name: density
arguments.description: The density of the fixture.

returns.name: fixture
returns.description: The new fixture.

arguments.description: The x position of the anchor point.

arguments.name: y

arguments.name: x2

arguments.name: y2

entries.name: newGearJoint
entries.description:
Create a GearJoint connecting two Joints.

The gear joint connects two joints that must be either  prismatic or  revolute joints. Using this joint requires that the joints it uses connect their respective bodies to the ground and have the ground as the first body. When destroying the bodies and joints you must make sure you destroy the gear joint before the other joints.

The gear joint has a ratio the determines how the angular or distance values of the connected joints relate to each other. The formula coordinate1 + ratio * coordinate2 always has a constant value that is set when the gear joint is created.

arguments.name: joint1
arguments.description: The first joint to connect with a gear joint.

arguments.name: joint2
arguments.description: The second joint to connect with a gear joint.

arguments.name: ratio
arguments.description: The gear ratio.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new gear joint.

entries.name: newMotorJoint
entries.description:
Creates a joint between two bodies which controls the relative motion between them.

Position and rotation offsets can be specified once the MotorJoint has been created, as well as the maximum motor force and torque that will be be applied to reach the target offsets.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: correctionFactor
arguments.description: The joint's initial position correction factor, in the range of 1.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new MotorJoint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to attach to the joint.

arguments.name: correctionFactor
arguments.description: The joint's initial position correction factor, in the range of 1.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new MotorJoint.

entries.name: newMouseJoint
entries.description:
Create a joint between a body and the mouse.

This joint actually connects the body to a fixed point in the world. To make it follow the mouse, the fixed point must be updated every timestep (example below).

The advantage of using a MouseJoint instead of just changing a body position directly is that collisions and reactions to other joints are handled by the physics engine.

arguments.name: body
arguments.description: The body to attach to the mouse.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x position of the connecting point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y position of the connecting point.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new mouse joint.

entries.name: newPolygonShape
entries.description:
Creates a new PolygonShape.

This shape can have 8 vertices at most, and must form a convex shape.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x position of the first point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y position of the first point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x position of the second point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y position of the second point.

arguments.name: x3
arguments.description: The x position of the third point.

arguments.name: y3
arguments.description: The y position of the third point.
You can continue passing more point positions to create the PolygonShape.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: A new PolygonShape.

arguments.name: vertices
arguments.description: A list of vertices to construct the polygon, in the form of {x1, y1, x2, y2, x3, y3, ...}.

returns.name: shape
returns.description: A new PolygonShape.

entries.name: newPrismaticJoint
entries.description:
Creates a PrismaticJoint between two bodies.

A prismatic joint constrains two bodies to move relatively to each other on a specified axis. It does not allow for relative rotation. Its definition and operation are similar to a  revolute joint, but with translation and force substituted for angle and torque.

arguments.description: The first body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: x
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the anchor point.

arguments.name: y
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the anchor point.

arguments.name: ax
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the axis vector.

arguments.name: ay
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the axis vector.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: ax
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the axis unit vector.

arguments.name: ay
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the axis unit vector.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a prismatic joint.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the first anchor point.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the second anchor point.

arguments.name: ax
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the axis unit vector.

arguments.name: ay
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the axis unit vector.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

arguments.name: referenceAngle
arguments.description: The reference angle between body1 and body2, in radians.

returns.description: The new prismatic joint.

entries.name: newPulleyJoint
entries.description:
Creates a PulleyJoint to join two bodies to each other and the ground.

The pulley joint simulates a pulley with an optional block and tackle. If the ratio parameter has a value different from one, then the simulated rope extends faster on one side than the other. In a pulley joint the total length of the simulated rope is the constant length1 + ratio * length2, which is set when the pulley joint is created.

Pulley joints can behave unpredictably if one side is fully extended. It is recommended that the method  setMaxLengths  be used to constrain the maximum lengths each side can attain.

arguments.name: body1
arguments.description: The first body to connect with a pulley joint.

arguments.name: body2
arguments.description: The second body to connect with a pulley joint.

arguments.name: gx1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the first body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: gy1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the first body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: gx2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the second body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: gy2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the second body's ground anchor.

arguments.name: x1
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the first body.

arguments.name: y1
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the first body.

arguments.name: x2
arguments.description: The x coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the second body.

arguments.name: y2
arguments.description: The y coordinate of the pulley joint anchor in the second body.

arguments.name: ratio
arguments.description: The joint ratio.

arguments.name: collideConnected
arguments.description: Specifies whether the two bodies should collide with each other.

returns.name: joint
returns.description: The new pulley joint.
